  • Can tests run repeatedly without flakes?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with API End-to-End Tests.

docs.title: Testcontainers CI Considerations
docs.text:
Running Testcontainers in CI requires balancing reliability, runtime, and cost. Integration jobs are often the most realistic checks in your pipeline, but also the most resource-sensitive.

This guide explains how to keep container-based tests stable and actionable in automated environments.

docs.title: Running Tests in CI/CD
docs.text: Start with a minimal workflow that installs Go, checks out code, and runs tests with explicit timeout boundaries.

docs.title: GitHub Actions Example
docs.text:
name: Integration Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Run tests with Testcontainers
        run: go test -v -race -timeout 10m ./...

For larger repositories, split fast unit checks and heavier integration checks into separate jobs.

docs.title: Docker-in-Docker (DinD)
docs.text:
DinD is sometimes required in containerized CI runners, but it adds complexity and should be used deliberately.

For running Testcontainers in CI containers:

services:
  docker:
    image: docker:dind
    options: --privileged

steps:
  - run: go test -v ./...

Prefer native Docker-capable runners when available, since they are often simpler and faster.

docs.title: Performance Optimization
docs.text: Optimize for stable feedback first, then reduce runtime and cost.

docs.title: Reuse Containers
docs.text:
const skipCleanup = os.Getenv("TESTCONTAINERS_SKIP_CLEANUP") != ""

func setupDatabase(t *testing.T) *sql.DB {
    // Setup code

    if !skipCleanup {
        t.Cleanup(func() {
            container.Terminate(ctx)
        })
    }
}

Use reuse/skip-cleanup controls cautiously. They can improve speed but may increase cross-test state risk.

docs.title: Parallel Test Execution
docs.text:
go test -parallel 4 ./...

func TestDatabaseOperations(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()
    // Test code
}

Parallelization must match runner CPU/memory limits to avoid noisy failures.

docs.title: Resource Limits
docs.text: Resource constraints prevent runaway jobs and improve CI predictability.

docs.title: Container Resource Constraints
docs.text:
req := testcontainers.ContainerRequest{
    Image: "postgres:15",
    HostConfigModifier: func(hc *container.HostConfig) {
        hc.Memory = 512 * 1024 * 1024  // 512MB
        hc.MemorySwap = 512 * 1024 * 1024
        hc.CPUQuota = 100000
    },
}

docs.title: Network Issues
docs.text: Container startup and network readiness are common CI flake sources.

docs.title: Timeouts
docs.text:
wait.ForListeningPort("5432/tcp").WithTimeout(30 * time.Second)

Set timeouts intentionally for CI variance, not just local machine speed.

docs.title: Health Checks
docs.text:
wait.ForLog("ready to accept connections").WithOccurrence(2)

Use health conditions that reflect real readiness, not just process start.

docs.title: Cost Optimization for CI
docs.text:
1. Cache Docker Images: Pull images once, reuse
  2. Parallel Tests: Use t.Parallel()
  3. Test Timeouts: Set reasonable timeouts
  4. Cleanup: Always cleanup containers

docs.title: Debugging CI Failures
docs.text: Diagnostics should be first-class in integration pipelines.

docs.title: Enable Container Logs
docs.text:
req := testcontainers.ContainerRequest{
    Image: "postgres:15",
    LogConsumerCfg: &testcontainers.LogConsumerConfig{
        Consumers: []testcontainers.LogConsumer{
            &testcontainers.StdoutLogConsumer{},
        },
    },
}

Log capture drastically reduces mean-time-to-resolution for intermittent failures.

docs.title: Save Logs from Failed Tests
docs.text:
docker logs <container-id> > /tmp/container.log

In CI, publish logs as artifacts so failures are diagnosable post-run.

docs.title: Environment-Specific Configuration
docs.text:
func isCI() bool {
    return os.Getenv("CI") != ""
}

func getTestTimeout() time.Duration {
    if isCI() {
        return 30 * time.Second
    }
    return 10 * time.Second
}

Keep CI-specific tuning centralized to avoid hidden environment conditionals spread across tests.

docs.title: Monitoring and Alerts
docs.text:
• Monitor CI test duration trends
  • Alert on repeated failures
  • Track container startup times
  • Monitor Docker daemon health

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Containerized Dependency Sanity Test
docs.text:
Goal: Validate app behavior against a real dependency in a container.

  1. Start a dependency container (for example PostgreSQL) in test setup.
  2. Run migration/init step.
  3. Execute one create + one read test case.
  4. Tear down container cleanly after test run.

Stretch: Add a test that verifies behavior when container startup is delayed.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Test Isolation and Cleanup Validation
docs.text:
Goal: Prevent flaky integration tests.

  1. Seed test data with unique IDs per test case.
  2. Ensure each test cleans up created records/resources.
  3. Run tests multiple times and assert stable outcomes.
  4. Add one assertion for no leaked state between tests.

Stretch: Run tests in parallel with isolated resources.

docs.title: Assignment: CI Job for Bookshelf Integration Tests
Goal
docs.text:
Run container-based integration tests in GitHub Actions reliably.

This assignment formalizes integration checks as a visible quality gate in pull requests.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create workflow file .github/workflows/integration-tests.yml.
  2. Add separate job for integration tests:
name: Bookshelf Integration Tests

on:
    pull_request:
    push:
        branches: [ main ]

jobs:
    integration:
        runs-on: ubuntu-latest
        steps:
            - uses: actions/checkout@v4
            - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
              with:
                go-version: "1.23"
            - name: Run integration tests
              run: go test -v -timeout 15m ./tests/integration/...
  1. Keep unit tests and integration tests as separate jobs.

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• PRs show explicit integration test status
  • Timeout is high enough for container startup variability

Also ensure failed runs provide logs/artifacts for rapid triage.

docs.title: Deep Dive: CI Stability and Cost Control
Background
docs.text:
Container-based tests can be the most expensive part of CI. Stability and cost optimization should be designed together.

Reliable pipelines are cheaper in practice because they reduce reruns and debugging churn.

docs.title: Stability Practices
docs.text:
1. Separate unit and integration jobs.
  2. Use explicit test timeouts.
  3. Capture container logs on failure.

  4. Avoid over-parallelization when Docker resources are limited.

  5. Keep integration suite scope focused on behaviors requiring real infrastructure.

docs.title: Cost Practices
docs.text:
1. Trigger integration jobs on PR and main only.
  2. Reuse pulled images via CI cache where possible.
  3. Keep integration suites scoped to behavior that truly needs real infrastructure.

docs.title: Example Failure Artifact Step
docs.text:
- name: Upload integration diagnostics
    if: failure()
    uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
    with:
        name: integration-diagnostics
        path: |
            **/test-results/*.log
            **/coverage-integration.out

docs.title: SDET KPI Ideas
docs.text:
• Integration job pass rate
  • Median integration runtime
  • Container startup duration trend

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Running all tests in one monolithic CI job.
  • Missing timeout boundaries for long-running integration suites.
  • Failing without publishing container/test diagnostics.
  • Aggressive parallelization on under-provisioned runners.

docs.title: Quick CI Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
• Are unit and integration jobs separated?
  • Are timeouts explicit and realistic?
  • Are container logs captured on failures?
  • Are runner resource limits considered for parallel tests?
  • Is integration scope limited to high-value infrastructure behaviors?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Why Contract Testing?.

docs.title: Consumer Contract Tests
docs.text:
Consumer contract tests validate whether a client can safely integrate with a provider contract before provider deployment. They protect the consumer experience by checking API assumptions close to client code.

Treat this suite as consumer behavior verification, not as a replacement for full API integration tests.

docs.title: Writing Consumer Contract Tests
docs.text: A reliable consumer suite should include deterministic interaction setup, meaningful assertion messages, and reusable client helpers.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
package myapp_test

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"
    "net/http"
    "testing"

    "github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2/consumer"
    "github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2/matchers"
)

func TestUserServiceConsumerContract(t *testing.T) {
    pact := consumer.NewPact(
        "FrontendApp",
        "UserService",
        consumer.WithPort(8082),
    )
    defer pact.WritePact()

    // Define all interactions
    defineUserServiceInteractions(pact)

    // Execute test against mock provider
    err := pact.ExecuteTest(t, func(cfg *consumer.MockServerConfig) error {
        client := NewUserServiceClient(cfg.URL)

        // Test 1: Get user
        user, err := client.GetUser(1)
        if err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("GetUser failed: %w", err)
        }
        if user.Name == "" {
            return fmt.Errorf("user name is empty")
        }

        // Test 2: List users
        users, err := client.ListUsers()
        if err != nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("ListUsers failed: %w", err)
        }
        if len(users) == 0 {
            return fmt.Errorf("users list is empty")
        }

        return nil
    })

    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("consumer test failed: %v", err)
    }
}

func defineUserServiceInteractions(pact *consumer.Pact) {
    // Interaction 1: Get user
    pact.AddInteraction().
        Given("user with id 1 exists").
        UponReceiving("a request to get user 1").
        WithRequest("GET", "/api/users/1").
        WithHeader("Accept", "application/json").
        WillRespondWith(200).
        WithHeader("Content-Type", "application/json").
        WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
            "id":    matchers.Integer(),
            "name":  matchers.String(),
            "email": matchers.String(),
        })

    // Interaction 2: List users
    pact.AddInteraction().
        Given("multiple users exist").
        UponReceiving("a request to list all users").
        WithRequest("GET", "/api/users").
        WillRespondWith(200).
        WithHeader("Content-Type", "application/json").
        WithBody(matchers.EachLike(map[string]interface{}{
            "id":    matchers.Integer(),
            "name":  matchers.String(),
            "email": matchers.String(),
        }, 1))
}

Keep interaction descriptions human-readable because they become part of triage output.

docs.title: Testing Different Scenarios
docs.text: Cover one happy path and at least one failure path per endpoint family.

docs.title: Success Cases
docs.text:
pact.AddInteraction().
    Given("valid user data").
    UponReceiving("a POST request to create user").
    WithRequest("POST", "/api/users").
    WithHeader("Content-Type", "application/json").
    WithBody(map[string]string{
        "name":  "John",
        "email": "john@example.com",
    }).
    WillRespondWith(201).
    WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
        "id":    matchers.Integer(),
        "name":  "John",
        "email": "john@example.com",
    })

docs.title: Error Cases
docs.text:
pact.AddInteraction().
    Given("user not found").
    UponReceiving("a GET request for non-existent user").
    WithRequest("GET", "/api/users/999").
    WillRespondWith(404).
    WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
        "error": matchers.String(),
    })

pact.AddInteraction().
    Given("invalid input").
    UponReceiving("a POST request with invalid data").
    WithRequest("POST", "/api/users").
    WithBody(map[string]string{
        "name": "",
    }).
    WillRespondWith(400).
    WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
        "error": matchers.String(),
    })

docs.title: Using Matchers
docs.text:
matchers.Integer()           // Matches any integer
matchers.String()            // Matches any string
matchers.Boolean()           // Matches any boolean
matchers.Decimal()           // Matches decimal numbers
matchers.Regex(".*@.*")      // Matches regex pattern
matchers.Equality("exact")   // Exact match
matchers.IncludesKey("id")   // Map includes key
matchers.EachLike(...)       // Array of similar items
matchers.Uuid()              // UUID format

Use strict equality only when exact values are part of the API guarantee.

docs.title: Table-Driven Consumer Tests
docs.text:
Table-driven style can reduce repetition, but keep behavior boundaries clear.

func TestUserServiceConsumerInteractions(t *testing.T) {
    pact := consumer.NewPact(
        "FrontendApp",
        "UserService",
        consumer.WithPort(8083),
    )
    defer pact.WritePact()

    tests := []struct {
        name     string
        setup    func(*consumer.Pact)
        test     func(string) error
    }{
        {
            name: "get user",
            setup: func(p *consumer.Pact) {
                p.AddInteraction().
                    Given("user exists").
                    UponReceiving("get user").
                    WithRequest("GET", "/api/users/1").
                    WillRespondWith(200).
                    WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
                        "id":   1,
                        "name": matchers.String(),
                    })
            },
            test: func(baseURL string) error {
                client := NewUserServiceClient(baseURL)
                _, err := client.GetUser(1)
                return err
            },
        },
    }

    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            tt.setup(pact)
        })
    }

    pact.ExecuteTest(t, func(cfg *consumer.MockServerConfig) error {
        for _, tt := range tests {
            if err := tt.test(cfg.URL); err != nil {
                return fmt.Errorf("%s failed: %w", tt.name, err)
            }
        }
        return nil
    })
}

If tables become too large, split by endpoint group for maintainability.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Define realistic interactions
  • Use matchers for flexibility
  • Test both success and failure paths
  • Keep contracts simple and focused
  • Review generated pact files

Additional guidance:

  • Prefer endpoint-specific helpers over global interaction builders.
  • Include validation and authorization failures where relevant.
  • Keep provider states explicit and deterministic.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Consumer Contract for Error Cases
docs.text:
Goal: Capture both happy and failure behavior in the contract.

  1. Define consumer expectations for one success response.
  2. Add at least two error interactions (validation + not found).
  3. Verify status code, headers, and payload fields.
  4. Publish or store contract artifact.

Stretch: Add backward-compatible optional field and update expectations.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Provider Verification in CI Style
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure provider remains contract-compatible.

  1. Load latest contract artifact in provider test stage.
  2. Verify provider responses satisfy all interactions.
  3. Fail verification on payload drift.
  4. Print actionable mismatch diagnostics.

Stretch: Add branch-based contract version selection.

docs.title: Assignment: Consumer Contract Suite for Bookshelf
Goal
docs.text: Create an incremental consumer contract suite aligned to the tutorial app features.

docs.title: Test Plan
docs.text:
1. GET /api/books returns list.
  2. POST /api/users creates a user.
  3. POST /api/books creates a book.
  4. POST /api/users invalid body returns 400.

docs.title: File Layout
docs.text:
tests/
    contract/
        consumer/
            bookshelf_consumer_test.go
            test_client.go

docs.title: Suggested Subtests
docs.text:
t.Run("list books", ...)
t.Run("create user", ...)
t.Run("create book", ...)
t.Run("create user invalid payload", ...)

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: go test ./tests/contract/consumer -v

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• At least 4 interactions generated.
  • Matchers are used instead of hardcoding dynamic values.

Also verify pact files are committed or published according to team workflow.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Building Reliable Consumer Suites
Background
docs.text: Consumer tests should verify what the consuming client actually needs to function. They are not full API tests; they are dependency behavior tests.

docs.title: Practical suite design
docs.text:
1. Start with critical user journeys (list books, create user).
  2. Add one negative path per endpoint family.
  3. Keep interaction naming consistent and human-readable.
  4. Avoid duplicating interaction setup logic by extracting helpers.

docs.title: Failure diagnosis pattern
docs.text:
When a consumer test fails, determine whether it is:

  • A client-side request mismatch (wrong path/header/body), or
  • A provider expectation mismatch (status/body shape).

This classification speeds up ownership and triage between teams.

docs.title: SDET practice
docs.text: Run consumer contract tests in PR checks for any client-side code changes that touch API integrations.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Asserting only status code without response shape expectations.
  • Reusing generic provider states that hide data intent.
  • Creating interactions for endpoints the client does not actually call.
  • Letting pact files drift from current client behavior.

docs.title: Quick Consumer Suite Checklist
docs.text:
• Are critical client journeys represented?
  • Are request and response expectations explicit?
  • Are dynamic values modeled with matchers?
  • Are negative interactions present for key endpoints?
  • Is pact output reviewed in CI or code review?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Provider Verification Tests.

docs.title: Pact Go Basics
docs.text:
Pact Go turns contract expectations into executable tests and portable pact files. Instead of manually coordinating integration assumptions, consumers define behaviors once and providers verify them continuously.

This section focuses on building high-signal interactions that remain stable as services evolve.

docs.title: What is Pact?
docs.text:
Pact is a contract testing framework that enables consumer-driven contract testing.

It provides a consumer-side mock server, contract generation, and provider verification workflow.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: go get github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2

docs.title: Pact
docs.text: Defines interactions between consumer and provider.

docs.title: Interaction
docs.text: A single request-response pair.

docs.title: Pact File
docs.text: JSON file containing all interactions (the "contract").

docs.title: Basic Terminology
docs.text:
• Consumer: Service that makes requests (e.g., Frontend, Mobile App)
  • Provider: Service that responds (e.g., API Server)
  • Interaction: Request/response pair
  • Verification: Provider confirms it matches contract

docs.title: Pact Consumer Test Structure
docs.text:
Think of this structure as four phases: define, execute, assert, and publish.

import "github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2/consumer"

func TestUserServiceConsumer(t *testing.T) {
    // 1. Create pact
    mockProvider := consumer.NewPact(
        "MyConsumer",
        "UserService",
        consumer.WithPort(8080),
    )

    // 2. Define expected interactions
    mockProvider.AddInteraction().
        Given("user with id 1 exists").
        UponReceiving("a request for user 1").
        WithRequest("GET", "/users/1").
        WillRespondWith(200, map[string]string{
            "Content-Type": "application/json",
        }).
        WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
            "id":   1,
            "name": "John",
        })

    // 3. Run tests with mock
    err := mockProvider.ExecuteTest(t, func(config *consumer.MockServerConfig) error {
        // Use mockProvider URL in tests
        // e.g., http://localhost:8080

        client := NewUserClient(config.URL)
        user, err := client.GetUser(1)
        return err
    })

    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("pact test failed: %v", err)
    }

    // 4. Write pact file
    mockProvider.WritePact()
}

A strong consumer test validates both client behavior and contract compatibility.

docs.title: Consumer Mock Server
docs.text: Simulates provider responses defined in interactions.

docs.title: Matchers
docs.text:
Flexible assertions that allow variations:

body := map[string]interface{}{
    "id":    matchers.Integer(),
    "name":  matchers.String(),
    "email": matchers.Regex(".*@.*\\.com"),
}

Prefer matchers for dynamic values to avoid brittle contracts.

docs.title: State Management
docs.text:
Setup data states for tests:

mockProvider.
    Given("user with id 1 exists").
    UponReceiving("a request for user 1")

docs.title: Example Consumer Test
docs.text:
This example demonstrates request headers, mock invocation, and status validation under Pact-managed expectations.

func TestGetUserConsumer(t *testing.T) {
    pact := consumer.NewPact(
        "FrontendApp",
        "UserAPI",
        consumer.WithPort(8081),
    )
    defer pact.WritePact()

    pact.AddInteraction().
        Given("user 1 exists").
        UponReceiving("a GET request for user 1").
        WithRequest("GET", "/api/users/1").
        WithHeader("Accept", "application/json").
        WillRespondWith(200).
        WithHeader("Content-Type", "application/json").
        WithBody(map[string]interface{}{
            "id":    1,
            "name":  matchers.String(),
            "email": matchers.String(),
        })

    err := pact.ExecuteTest(t, func(config *consumer.MockServerConfig) error {
        client := &http.Client{}
        req, _ := http.NewRequest("GET", config.URL+"/api/users/1", nil)
        req.Header.Set("Accept", "application/json")

        resp, err := client.Do(req)
        if err != nil {
            return err
        }
        defer resp.Body.Close()

        if resp.StatusCode != 200 {
            return fmt.Errorf("unexpected status: %d", resp.StatusCode)
        }

        return nil
    })

    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("test failed: %v", err)
    }
}

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Consumer Contract for Error Cases
docs.text:
Goal: Capture both happy and failure behavior in the contract.

  1. Define consumer expectations for one success response.
  2. Add at least two error interactions (validation + not found).
  3. Verify status code, headers, and payload fields.
  4. Publish or store contract artifact.

Stretch: Add backward-compatible optional field and update expectations.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Provider Verification in CI Style
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure provider remains contract-compatible.

  1. Load latest contract artifact in provider test stage.
  2. Verify provider responses satisfy all interactions.
  3. Fail verification on payload drift.
  4. Print actionable mismatch diagnostics.

Stretch: Add branch-based contract version selection.

docs.title: Assignment: Create First Bookshelf Pact Consumer Test
Goal
docs.text:
Generate a pact file from a consumer test for Bookshelf endpoints.

This assignment establishes the baseline contract artifact for downstream provider verification.

docs.title: Route Prefix Note
docs.text: Use the same route prefix as your running service for this stage (/api in tutorial sections, /api/v1 in capstone).

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Install pact-go:
go get github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2
  1. Create tests/contract/consumer/bookshelf_consumer_test.go.
  2. Define interactions for:
  3. GET /api/books success
  4. POST /api/users success
  5. POST /api/users validation failure

Starter interaction:

pact.AddInteraction().
    Given("books exist").
    UponReceiving("a request to list books").
    WithRequest("GET", "/api/books").
    WillRespondWith(200).
    WithHeader("Content-Type", "application/json").
    WithBody(matchers.EachLike(map[string]interface{}{
        "id":            matchers.String(),
        "title":         matchers.String(),
        "author":        matchers.String(),
        "isbn":          matchers.String(),
        "publishedYear": matchers.Integer(),
    }, 1))
  1. Run and generate pact files:
go test ./tests/contract/consumer -v

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Pact JSON is generated under pacts/.
  • Interactions map to real Bookshelf endpoints.

Also ensure interaction descriptions are readable in CI output and reports.

docs.title: Interaction quality checklist
docs.text:
1. Use precise provider states (books exist, no books exist).
  2. Include request headers that matter (Accept, Content-Type).
  3. Prefer matchers for dynamic values (IDs, timestamps, UUIDs).
  4. Add both happy-path and validation-path interactions.

docs.title: Matcher philosophy
docs.text: Overly strict payload matching creates fragile contracts. Use type and format matchers so legitimate provider evolution does not break consumers.

docs.title: Example matcher intent
docs.text:
"id": matchers.String(),
"publishedYear": matchers.Integer(),

This validates interface compatibility while avoiding brittle literal-value checks.

docs.title: SDET tip
docs.text: Keep one behavioral intent per interaction description so pact reports are easy to diagnose in CI.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Hardcoding dynamic IDs/timestamps instead of using matchers.
  • Combining unrelated assertions into one large interaction.
  • Omitting request headers that affect provider behavior.
  • Writing interactions that do not map to real client calls.

docs.title: Quick Pact Authoring Checklist
docs.text:
• Does each interaction represent one clear behavior?
  • Are headers and path parameters explicitly modeled?
  • Are matchers used for non-deterministic fields?
  • Are both success and failure scenarios included?
  • Will failure output be understandable to another team?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Consumer Contract Tests.

docs.title: Pact in CI
docs.text:
Contract testing becomes truly valuable when integrated into CI and release governance. Running Pact locally is useful, but automated publication, verification, and deploy decisions are what prevent production compatibility regressions.

This guide focuses on reliable CI workflow design for contract-based delivery.

docs.title: Pact Broker
docs.text:
The Pact Broker is a central repository for pact files and verification results.

It acts as the source of truth for compatibility history across teams and versions.

# Run Pact Broker via Docker
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 pactfoundation/pact-broker:latest

docs.title: Publishing Consumer Pacts
docs.text: Consumer pipelines should publish pact artifacts every time API-calling behavior changes.

docs.title: GitHub Actions: Consumer Job
docs.text:
name: Consumer Contract Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Run consumer tests
        run: go test -v ./tests/consumer

      - name: Publish pacts
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}/pacts:/pacts \
            pactfoundation/pact-cli:latest \
            publish /pacts \
            --consumer-app-version=${{ github.sha }} \
            --broker-base-url=http://pact-broker:8080 \
            --branch=${{ github.ref_name }}

docs.title: Provider Verification in CI
docs.text: Provider pipelines should fetch current relevant pacts and verify against the running provider.

docs.title: GitHub Actions: Provider Job
docs.text:
name: Provider Verification

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    services:
      pact-broker:
        image: pactfoundation/pact-broker:latest
        ports:
          - 8080:8080

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Download pacts from broker
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}/pacts:/pacts \
            --network host \
            pactfoundation/pact-cli:latest \
            find --broker-base-url=http://localhost:8080 \
            --output=/pacts \
            --consumer-version-selectors='latest' \
            -a 'UserService'

      - name: Run provider
        run: go run ./cmd/server &

      - name: Verify pacts
        run: go test -v ./tests/provider -run TestProviderVerification

      - name: Publish verification
        run: |
          docker run --rm \
            -v ${{ github.workspace }}:/app \
            --network host \
            pactfoundation/pact-cli:latest \
            publish-provider-results \
            --broker-base-url=http://localhost:8080 \
            --provider-app-version=${{ github.sha }} \
            --branch=${{ github.ref_name }}

docs.title: CLI Publishing
docs.text: CLI commands are useful for local checks, scripted pipelines, and emergency manual recovery workflows.

docs.title: Publish Consumer Pacts
docs.text:
pact-cli publish ./pacts \
  --consumer-app-version=1.0.0 \
  --broker-base-url=http://pact-broker:8080 \
  --branch=main

docs.title: Publish Provider Results
docs.text:
pact-cli publish-provider-results ./verification-results \
  --provider-app-version=1.0.0 \
  --broker-base-url=http://pact-broker:8080 \
  --branch=main

docs.title: Programmatic Publishing
docs.text:
Programmatic publishing is useful when you want publication tightly coupled to test execution code.

import "github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2/publisher"

func TestPublishPacts(t *testing.T) {
    err := publisher.Publish(
        "frontend-app",
        "1.0.0",
        []string{"pacts/"},
        publisher.WithBrokerURL("http://pact-broker:8080"),
        publisher.WithBrokerToken("token"),
        publisher.WithBranchName("main"),
    )
    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("publish failed: %v", err)
    }
}

docs.title: Matrix Testing
docs.text:
Test multiple provider versions:

jobs:
  verify:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        go-version: ['1.20', '1.21', '1.22']
        provider-version: ['1.0.0', '2.0.0']

    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: ${{ matrix.go-version }}

      - name: Verify provider
        run: |
          go test -v ./tests/provider \
            -provider-version=${{ matrix.provider-version }}

Use matrix breadth carefully to balance coverage and CI runtime.

docs.title: Can I Deploy Decision
docs.text:
The Pact Broker can determine if it's safe to deploy:

pact-cli can-i-deploy \
  --broker-base-url=http://pact-broker:8080 \
  --pacticipant=UserService \
  --version=1.0.0 \
  --branch=main

Treat this check as a release gate, not an optional informational step.

In CI:

- name: Check if safe to deploy
  run: |
    docker run --rm \
      --network host \
      pactfoundation/pact-cli:latest \
      can-i-deploy \
      --broker-base-url=http://localhost:8080 \
      --pacticipant=UserService \
      --version=${{ github.sha }} \
      --branch=${{ github.ref_name }}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Publish all contracts to broker
  • Verify against broker-fetched pacts
  • Use versioning and branches
  • Monitor pact verification trends
  • Fail deployment if verification fails
  • Review contracts regularly

Additional guidance:

  • Use immutable app versions such as commit SHA.
  • Persist pact and verification artifacts for failed runs.
  • Keep broker connectivity checks explicit in pipeline setup.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Consumer Contract for Error Cases
docs.text:
Goal: Capture both happy and failure behavior in the contract.

  1. Define consumer expectations for one success response.
  2. Add at least two error interactions (validation + not found).
  3. Verify status code, headers, and payload fields.
  4. Publish or store contract artifact.

Stretch: Add backward-compatible optional field and update expectations.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Provider Verification in CI Style
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure provider remains contract-compatible.

  1. Load latest contract artifact in provider test stage.
  2. Verify provider responses satisfy all interactions.
  3. Fail verification on payload drift.
  4. Print actionable mismatch diagnostics.

Stretch: Add branch-based contract version selection.

docs.title: Assignment: Add Bookshelf Contract Tests to CI
Goal
docs.text:
Automate contract publication and provider verification in GitHub Actions.

This assignment establishes continuous compatibility governance for the Bookshelf project.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add .github/workflows/contract-tests.yml with two jobs:
  2. consumer-contract
  3. provider-verification
  4. Consumer job:
  5. runs go test ./tests/contract/consumer -v
  6. publishes pacts to broker (or stores as artifact initially)
  7. Provider job:
  8. starts provider app
  9. fetches pacts (broker or artifact)
  10. runs go test ./tests/contract/provider -v
  11. Add can-i-deploy gate before release.

Minimal workflow commands:

go test ./tests/contract/consumer -v
go test ./tests/contract/provider -v

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• PRs fail if consumer or provider contract tests fail.
  • Release pipeline checks can-i-deploy before deployment.

Also ensure failure diagnostics are retained as downloadable artifacts.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Contract testing becomes high-value only when integrated into deployment governance. The broker history plus can-i-deploy provides traceable compatibility decisions.

docs.title: Governance pipeline sequence
docs.text:
1. Run consumer tests and publish pacts.
  2. Run provider verification against current relevant pacts.
  3. Publish verification results.
  4. Execute can-i-deploy for release candidate.

docs.title: Operational safeguards
docs.text:
1. Use immutable app versions (git sha).
  2. Include branch/environment tags where needed.
  3. Fail fast on missing pact files or broker connectivity errors.
  4. Preserve pact/verification artifacts for debugging.

docs.title: SDET metrics to monitor
docs.text:
• Contract verification pass rate by provider version.
  • Mean time to fix contract breakages.
  • Number of blocked deployments due to can-i-deploy.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Publishing pacts without running corresponding consumer tests.
  • Verifying providers against stale local pact files instead of broker source.
  • Skipping can-i-deploy in release workflows.
  • Treating contract failures as flaky and rerunning without analysis.

docs.title: Quick Contract CI Checklist
docs.text:
• Are consumer pacts published per relevant change?
  • Does provider verification fetch current broker contracts?
  • Are verification results published back to broker?
  • Is can-i-deploy enforced before release?
  • Are diagnostics/artifacts available for failed runs?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with GitHub Actions for Go.

docs.title: Provider Verification Tests
docs.text:
Provider verification ensures your service implementation satisfies consumer contracts before release. It is the provider-side compatibility gate that prevents breaking downstream clients.

This page emphasizes deterministic provider state setup and realistic verification execution.

docs.title: What is Provider Verification?
docs.text:
Provider verification confirms that your service meets all contracts defined by consumers.

A passing verification means the provider can honor each published interaction under expected states.

docs.title: Basic Provider Test
docs.text:
The baseline pattern is: load pact files, expose provider endpoint, map provider states, run verification.

package myapp_test

import (
    "testing"

    "github.com/pact-foundation/pact-go/v2/provider"
)

func TestProviderVerification(t *testing.T) {
    verifier := provider.NewVerifier()

    err := verifier.VerifyProvider(t, provider.VerifyRequest{
        ProviderName:    "UserService",
        ProviderVersion: "1.0.0",

        // Path to pact files from consumer
        PactFiles: []string{
            "pacts/FrontendApp-UserService.json",
        },

        // Provider details
        Provider: "http://localhost:8080",

        // Map consumer states to setup functions
        StateHandlers: provider.StateHandlers{
            "user with id 1 exists": func() error {
                return setupUser(1, "John", "john@example.com")
            },
            "multiple users exist": func() error {
                return setupUsers()
            },
        },
    })

    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("verification failed: %v", err)
    }
}

func setupUser(id int, name, email string) error {
    // Insert test data into database
    db := getTestDB()
    _, err := db.Exec(`
        INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES ($1, $2, $3)
    `, id, name, email)
    return err
}

func setupUsers() error {
    // Insert multiple test users
    db := getTestDB()
    _, err := db.Exec(`
        INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES
            (1, 'John', 'john@example.com'),
            (2, 'Jane', 'jane@example.com'),
            (3, 'Bob', 'bob@example.com')
    `)
    return err
}

State handler quality usually determines whether verification is stable or flaky.

docs.title: With Testcontainers
docs.text:
Combine provider verification with Testcontainers for realistic testing:

func TestProviderWithDatabase(t *testing.T) {
    ctx := context.Background()

    // Start database container
    container, err := postgres.RunContainer(ctx,
        testcontainers.WithImage("postgres:15"),
        postgres.WithDatabase("testdb"),
        postgres.WithUsername("postgres"),
        postgres.WithPassword("password"),
    )
    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("failed to start postgres: %v", err)
    }
    defer container.Terminate(ctx)

    connStr, _ := container.ConnectionString(ctx)
    db, _ := sql.Open("postgres", connStr)
    defer db.Close()

    // Start API server
    server := startTestServer(db)
    defer server.Close()

    // Verify provider
    verifier := provider.NewVerifier()

    err = verifier.VerifyProvider(t, provider.VerifyRequest{
        ProviderName: "UserService",
        PactFiles:    []string{"pacts/consumer-userservice.json"},
        Provider:     server.URL,
        StateHandlers: provider.StateHandlers{
            "user with id 1 exists": func() error {
                return db.Exec(`
                    INSERT INTO users (id, name, email)
                    VALUES (1, 'John', 'john@example.com')
                `).Err
            },
        },
    })

    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("verification failed: %v", err)
    }
}

This setup is ideal when endpoint behavior depends on real database semantics.

docs.title: Multiple Pact Files
docs.text:
As consumer count grows, verifying all relevant pact files becomes a release requirement.

verifier := provider.NewVerifier()

err := verifier.VerifyProvider(t, provider.VerifyRequest{
    ProviderName: "UserService",
    Provider:     "http://localhost:8080",

    // Multiple consumers' pacts
    PactFiles: []string{
        "pacts/frontend-userservice.json",
        "pacts/mobile-userservice.json",
        "pacts/admin-userservice.json",
    },

    StateHandlers: provider.StateHandlers{
        "user exists":       setupUser,
        "users list ready":  setupUsers,
        "user not found":    cleanupUsers,
    },
})

docs.title: Complex State Handlers
docs.text:
Keep handlers focused, deterministic, and isolated to one interaction context.

StateHandlers: provider.StateHandlers{
    "user with id 1 exists and has premium status": func() error {
        user := &User{
            ID:     1,
            Name:   "John",
            Email:  "john@example.com",
            Status: "premium",
        }
        return saveUser(user)
    },

    "no users exist": func() error {
        return truncateUsersTable()
    },

    "orders exist for user 1": func() error {
        if err := setupUser(1, "John", "john@example.com"); err != nil {
            return err
        }
        order := &Order{
            ID:     1,
            UserID: 1,
            Total:  99.99,
        }
        return saveOrder(order)
    },
}

docs.title: Tags for Versioning
docs.text:
For multiple versions of the same provider:

err := verifier.VerifyProvider(t, provider.VerifyRequest{
    ProviderName:    "UserService",
    ProviderVersion: "2.0.0",
    PactFiles:       []string{"pacts/consumer-userservice.json"},
    Provider:        "http://localhost:8080",
    PublishVerificationResult: true,
    VersionTags:     []string{"2.0.0", "prod"},
})

Version and tag metadata improves broker history and deployment decision quality.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use realistic state handlers
  • Clean up data between interactions
  • Verify against production-like setup
  • Test all consumer pacts
  • Include error scenarios
  • Document state handler setup

Additional guidance:

  • Reset mutable tables between state setups.
  • Keep test fixture values explicit and stable.
  • Ensure provider app configuration matches contract environment assumptions.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Consumer Contract for Error Cases
docs.text:
Goal: Capture both happy and failure behavior in the contract.

  1. Define consumer expectations for one success response.
  2. Add at least two error interactions (validation + not found).
  3. Verify status code, headers, and payload fields.
  4. Publish or store contract artifact.

Stretch: Add backward-compatible optional field and update expectations.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Provider Verification in CI Style
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure provider remains contract-compatible.

  1. Load latest contract artifact in provider test stage.
  2. Verify provider responses satisfy all interactions.
  3. Fail verification on payload drift.
  4. Print actionable mismatch diagnostics.

Stretch: Add branch-based contract version selection.

docs.title: Assignment: Verify Bookshelf Provider Against Consumer Pacts
Goal
docs.text:
Run provider verification for Bookshelf API against generated pact files.

This assignment creates the provider-side contract gate for Bookshelf release confidence.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create tests/contract/provider/bookshelf_provider_test.go.
  2. Start Bookshelf API test server in test setup.
  3. Wire state handlers for pact states:
  4. books exist
  5. user can be created
  6. invalid user payload
  7. Verify pacts from pacts/.

Skeleton:

err := verifier.VerifyProvider(t, provider.VerifyRequest{
    ProviderName: "bookshelf-api",
    Provider:     server.URL,
    PactFiles: []string{
        "pacts/reading-ui-bookshelf-api.json",
    },
    StateHandlers: provider.StateHandlers{
        "books exist": func() error {
            return seedBooks(db)
        },
        "user can be created": func() error {
            return clearUsers(db)
        },
        "invalid user payload": func() error {
            return nil
        },
    },
})

docs.title: Verification
docs.text: go test ./tests/contract/provider -v

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Provider verification passes locally.
  • State handlers are deterministic and isolated.

Also verify failures produce enough diagnostics to identify the mismatched interaction quickly.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Provider State Handler Discipline
Why state handlers are critical
docs.text: Provider verification quality depends on deterministic setup for every interaction. Flaky state setup causes false negatives and erodes trust in contract checks.

docs.title: Deterministic handler rules
docs.text:
1. Seed only data needed for one interaction.
  2. Reset mutable tables between interactions.
  3. Use fixed test values and explicit IDs where possible.
  4. Avoid hidden external dependencies in setup logic.

docs.title: Verification depth recommendations
docs.text:
• Verify both success and failure interactions.
  • Include validation and not-found responses.
  • Ensure error envelope structure matches consumer expectations.

docs.title: SDET tip
docs.text: Treat provider verification as a release gate equivalent to integration tests for API compatibility.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Using state handlers that depend on pre-existing mutable data.
  • Verifying only one pact when multiple consumers exist.
  • Starting provider with configuration that differs from contract assumptions.
  • Ignoring verification output details and rerunning without root-cause analysis.

docs.title: Quick Provider Verification Checklist
docs.text:
• Are all relevant consumer pacts included?
  • Are state handlers deterministic and isolated?
  • Are success and error interactions both verified?
  • Is provider startup environment representative?
  • Are failures actionable from logs and reports?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Pact in CI.

docs.title: Why Contract Testing?
docs.text:
Contract testing is a scaling strategy for service integration confidence. It reduces the need to run every consumer-provider combination in full end-to-end environments, while still validating API compatibility before release.

For SDETs, this means faster feedback loops and clearer ownership when integration behavior changes.

docs.title: The Problem: Integration Testing at Scale
docs.text:
With many microservices, full end-to-end tests become: - Slow and fragile - Expensive to maintain - Hard to debug failures - Dependent on service availability

As systems grow, integration drift often becomes a bigger risk than outright outages.

docs.title: What is Contract Testing?
docs.text:
Contract testing verifies that services can communicate correctly by validating the "contract" (expected API structure) between them.

In practice, it checks request paths, methods, status codes, and response shapes that consumers depend on.

docs.title: Synchronous
docs.text:
• REST API contracts
  • gRPC contracts

docs.title: Asynchronous
docs.text:
• Message contracts
  • Event contracts

docs.title: Consumer Contract
docs.text:
What the consumer (client) expects from the provider (server).

Consumer says: "I expect GET /users/123 to return {id: 123, name: string}"

docs.title: Provider Contract
docs.text:
What the provider actually provides.

Provider says: "I provide GET /users/123 and return {id, name, email}"

docs.title: Consumer-Driven Contract Testing
docs.text:
1. Consumer writes tests defining what they expect
  2. Tests generate contracts (expectations)
  3. Provider verifies their API matches contracts
  4. CI runs contract verification before deployment

This workflow allows independent teams to release safely without requiring synchronized deployments.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Fast: No need for full integration environments
  • Scalable: Decouple service testing
  • Shift Left: Find issues early
  • Documentation: Contracts document APIs
  • Confidence: Deploy independently

Most importantly, failures point directly to compatibility mismatches, improving triage speed.

docs.title: Pact Workflow
docs.text: Consumer Tests → Pact File (Contract) → Provider Verification

docs.title: Consumer (Frontend) Expects:
docs.text:
{
  "GET /api/users/123": {
    "status": 200,
    "body": {
      "id": 123,
      "name": "John"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Provider (Backend) Must Provide:
docs.text:
• Endpoint: GET /api/users/123
  • Returns 200 status
  • Includes at least id and name fields

docs.title: When to Use Contract Testing
docs.text:
✓ Multiple independent services ✓ Frequent deployments ✓ Service teams are separate ✓ Want independent testing

✗ Monolithic applications ✗ Tightly coupled services ✗ Rare deployments

Use this section as a decision gate, not a rule carved in stone.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Consumer Contract for Error Cases
docs.text:
Goal: Capture both happy and failure behavior in the contract.

  1. Define consumer expectations for one success response.
  2. Add at least two error interactions (validation + not found).
  3. Verify status code, headers, and payload fields.
  4. Publish or store contract artifact.

Stretch: Add backward-compatible optional field and update expectations.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Provider Verification in CI Style
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure provider remains contract-compatible.

  1. Load latest contract artifact in provider test stage.
  2. Verify provider responses satisfy all interactions.
  3. Fail verification on payload drift.
  4. Print actionable mismatch diagnostics.

Stretch: Add branch-based contract version selection.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Model the Bookshelf app as producer and consumer services so contract testing has a concrete target.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
• bookshelf-api is the provider for book and review endpoints.
  • reading-ui (or a separate client package) is the consumer.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Document two consumer expectations:
  2. GET /api/books returns an array with id, title, author, isbn, publishedYear.
  3. POST /api/users returns 201 with a user payload containing id and email.
  4. Add one negative contract expectation:
  5. invalid POST /api/users returns 400 and an error response.
  6. Create docs/CONTRACT_SCOPE.md with:
  7. provider name
  8. consumer name
  9. initial endpoints covered

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Contract scope doc exists and is agreed by both sides.
  • Endpoints chosen are stable and versioned for future changes.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Contracts as Change Safety Nets
Why this matters
docs.text: In distributed systems, many failures are not outages, but integration drift: one service changes a field, status code, or payload shape and silently breaks consumers. Contract tests catch this drift before release.

docs.title: What contract tests should validate
docs.text:
1. Resource path and method (GET /api/books).
  2. Status code expectations (200, 201, 400, 404).
  3. Required response structure and key fields.
  4. Error envelope shape for failure scenarios.

docs.title: What they should not over-constrain
docs.text:
• Internal provider implementation details.
  • Optional fields the consumer does not use.
  • Exact ordering unless contractually required.

docs.title: SDET guidance
docs.text: Design each contract as a user-observable behavior, not as an implementation snapshot. This keeps contracts stable across refactoring.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Writing contracts that mirror provider internals instead of consumer needs.
  • Over-constraining optional fields and making contracts brittle.
  • Skipping negative scenarios such as validation and not-found paths.
  • Treating contracts as one-time setup and not evolving them with features.

docs.title: Quick Boundary Checklist
docs.text:
• Is each contract tied to a real consumer behavior?
  • Are required fields/status codes clearly specified?
  • Are optional fields modeled with flexibility?
  • Are error responses covered for key workflows?
  • Can both consumer and provider teams explain ownership?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Pact Go Basics.

docs.title: Coverage and Thresholds
docs.text:
Coverage gating is a risk-control mechanism, not a vanity metric. The objective is to improve confidence in critical behavior while preventing silent test-quality regressions over time.

This guide explains how to measure coverage, enforce practical thresholds, and use coverage trends as part of CI governance.

docs.title: Measuring Coverage
docs.text: Start with reproducible commands that generate machine-readable output and human-friendly reports.

docs.title: Generate Coverage Report
docs.text: go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...

docs.title: View Coverage
docs.text: go tool cover -html=coverage.out

docs.title: Coverage by Function
docs.text:
go test -v -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
go tool cover -html=coverage.out -o coverage.html

Function-level views help identify high-risk code paths that lack test depth.

docs.title: Coverage Percentage
docs.text: go test -cover ./...

docs.title: Coverage Thresholds
docs.text: Thresholds should reflect risk profile, not arbitrary targets.

docs.title: GitHub Actions: Coverage Check
docs.text:
name: Coverage

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  coverage:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Generate coverage
        run: go test -v -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...

      - name: Check coverage
        run: |
          coverage=$(go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/%//')
          if (( $(echo "$coverage < 80" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "Coverage $coverage% is below 80% threshold"
            exit 1
          fi

Use this as a baseline and evolve to package-specific gates for critical modules.

docs.title: Using gcov2lcov
docs.text:
- name: Convert coverage to lcov
  run: |
    go install github.com/jandelgado/gcov2lcov@latest
    gcov2lcov -infile=coverage.out -outfile=coverage.lcov

- name: Upload coverage
  uses: coverallsapp/github-action@master
  with:
    github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    path-to-lcov: coverage.lcov

docs.title: Using Codecov
docs.text:
- name: Upload to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
  with:
    files: ./coverage.out
    flags: unittests
    fail_ci_if_error: true

docs.title: Per-Package Coverage Targets
docs.text:
Per-package targets prevent strong overall coverage from hiding weak critical components.

// Makefile
check-coverage:
    @go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
    @echo "Overall coverage:"
    @go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total
    @echo ""
    @echo "Package coverage:"
    @go tool cover -func=coverage.out | awk 'NR > 1 && $$NF < 80 {print $$1 ": " $$NF "%"}' | sort | uniq

docs.title: Exclude Files from Coverage
docs.text:
Use exclusions sparingly and document why each exclusion is justified.

// coverage_helper_test.go
// +build !coverage

package myapp_test

Or use:

go test -cover -covermode=count ./...

docs.title: Quality Gates
docs.text: A good quality gate fails fast with clear diagnostics and actionable context.

docs.title: Enforce Minimum Coverage
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Run tests with coverage
go test -v -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...

# Extract coverage percentage
coverage=$(go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/%//')
minimum=80

# Check threshold
if (( $(echo "$coverage < $minimum" | bc -l) )); then
    echo "FAIL: Coverage $coverage% is below $minimum%"
    exit 1
fi

echo "PASS: Coverage is $coverage%"

docs.title: Combined with Other Checks
docs.text:
jobs:
  quality:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Run tests
        run: go test -v -race ./...

      - name: Check coverage
        run: |
          go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
          coverage=$(go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/%//')
          if (( $(echo "$coverage < 80" | bc -l) )); then
            echo "Coverage $coverage% below 80%"
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Lint
        uses: golangci/golangci-lint-action@v3

      - name: Static analysis
        run: go vet ./...

Coverage works best when combined with race checks, linting, and static analysis.

docs.title: Reporting Coverage Trends
docs.text:
Trend reporting helps teams catch gradual regressions before they become release risk.

- name: Comment coverage on PR
  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
  uses: romeovs/lcov-reporter-action@v0.3.1
  with:
    lcov-file: ./coverage.lcov

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Set realistic coverage targets (80-90%)
  • Include edge cases in coverage
  • Monitor coverage trends over time
  • Don't aim for 100% - focus on critical paths
  • Review uncovered code regularly
  • Use incremental coverage gates

Additional guidance:

  • Track both global and critical-package thresholds.
  • Highlight uncovered hotspots in PR summaries.
  • Treat sudden drops as investigation triggers, not just numeric failures.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Quality Gate Pipeline Step
docs.text:
Goal: Enforce minimum quality before merge.

  1. Add pipeline steps for lint, unit tests, and coverage.
  2. Set a coverage threshold and fail when below threshold.
  3. Publish test and coverage reports as artifacts.
  4. Verify failure output is easy to diagnose.

Stretch: Add separate fast/slow test stages.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Safe Integration Test Stage
docs.text:
Goal: Keep CI reliable while running heavier tests.

  1. Add integration tests in a dedicated stage/job.
  2. Gate stage execution by branch or label rule.
  3. Capture logs and container output on failure.
  4. Ensure secrets are injected securely via CI variables.

Stretch: Add retry policy only for known flaky external dependency failures.

docs.title: Assignment: Coverage Gate Policy for Bookshelf
Goal
docs.text:
Enforce minimum test coverage and protect critical modules.

This assignment formalizes coverage as a merge gate for the Bookshelf project.

docs.title: Coverage Policy
docs.text:
• Global minimum: 80%
  • Domain package target (pkg/domain): 90%
  • Handler package target (pkg/handler): 85%

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Generate coverage in CI:
go test -v -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
go tool cover -func=coverage.out
  1. Add gate script scripts/check-coverage.sh:
  2. fail when total coverage < 80

  3. print uncovered hotspots

  4. Upload coverage artifact and optionally publish to Codecov.

  5. Add PR summary comment with total coverage and delta.

docs.title: Suggested Gate Script Logic
docs.text:
coverage=$(go tool cover -func=coverage.out | awk '/total:/ {print $3}' | sed 's/%//')
minimum=80
if (( $(echo "$coverage < $minimum" | bc -l) )); then
  echo "Coverage $coverage% is below $minimum%"
  exit 1
fi

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• CI fails automatically below threshold.
  • Team can track trend and prevent coverage regressions.

Also ensure threshold failures include clear instructions for contributors.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Coverage as Risk Management
Background
docs.text: Coverage is not a quality metric by itself; it is a visibility metric. The goal is to maximize confidence on critical paths, not to maximize percentages blindly.

docs.title: Practical threshold strategy
docs.text:
1. Set realistic global threshold (for example, 80%).
  2. Set higher thresholds for business-critical packages.
  3. Enforce non-regression: block significant drops from baseline.
  4. Pair coverage checks with mutation-prone area reviews.

docs.title: Interpreting low coverage hotspots
docs.text:
• Low coverage in error handling paths often indicates hidden production risk.
  • Low coverage in handlers can hide status-code and payload contract regressions.
  • Low coverage in concurrency code can hide race-related defects.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Track trend over time and prioritize adding tests where change frequency and business impact intersect.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Chasing 100% coverage while missing critical failure-path tests.
  • Relying only on global threshold and ignoring weak key packages.
  • Treating coverage drop failures as flaky instead of investigating root cause.
  • Excluding files without explicit rationale.

docs.title: Quick Coverage Gate Checklist
docs.text:
• Is coverage generated consistently in CI?
  • Are thresholds risk-based and documented?
  • Are critical packages tracked separately?
  • Are PRs showing trend/delta information?
  • Are failures actionable for contributors?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Linting and Static Analysis.

docs.title: GitHub Actions for Go
docs.text:
GitHub Actions pipelines are the operational backbone of Go quality gates. A well-structured workflow reduces feedback time, isolates failures clearly, and scales with team velocity.

This page focuses on practical workflow architecture for reliable Go CI.

docs.title: Basic Workflow
docs.text: Begin with one deterministic test job, then split responsibilities as the project grows.

docs.title: Simple Test Workflow
docs.text:
name: Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Go
        uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Run tests
        run: go test -v -race -timeout=10m ./...

docs.title: Matrix Testing
docs.text:
Test across multiple Go versions and operating systems:

jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        go-version: ['1.20', '1.21', '1.22']
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]

    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: ${{ matrix.go-version }}
      - run: go test -v ./...

Use matrix coverage strategically for compatibility-sensitive projects.

docs.title: Caching Dependencies
docs.text:
Effective caching reduces CI runtime and protects feedback loops as dependency graphs grow.

steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@v3
  - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
    with:
      go-version: '1.21'
  - uses: actions/cache@v3
    with:
      path: ~/go/pkg/mod
      key: ${{ runner.os }}-go-${{ hashFiles('**/go.sum') }}
      restore-keys: |
        ${{ runner.os }}-go-
  - run: go test -v ./...

docs.title: Build and Push
docs.text: Build and artifact steps should produce reproducible outputs tied to immutable commit identifiers.

docs.title: Build Binary
docs.text:
- name: Build
  run: go build -o app ./cmd/server

- name: Upload artifact
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
  with:
    name: app
    path: app

docs.title: Docker Build and Push
docs.text:
- name: Build and push Docker image
  uses: docker/build-push-action@v4
  with:
    context: .
    push: true
    tags: myregistry/myapp:${{ github.sha }}
    username: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_USERNAME }}
    password: ${{ secrets.DOCKER_PASSWORD }}

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Keep environment wiring explicit so test behavior is predictable and auditable.

env:
  ENVIRONMENT: test
  LOG_LEVEL: debug

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://user:pass@localhost/test

    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:15
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5
        ports:
          - 5432:5432

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
      - run: go test -v ./...

docs.title: Secrets
docs.text:
Secrets should only be injected at job scope where required and never echoed in logs.

- name: Run tests
  env:
    API_KEY: ${{ secrets.API_KEY }}
    SECRET_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SECRET_TOKEN }}
  run: go test -v ./...

docs.title: Outputs and Notifications
docs.text: Notifications and artifacts should improve triage, not create noise.

docs.title: Slack Notification
docs.text:
- name: Notify Slack
  if: always()
  uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
  with:
    webhook-url: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}
    payload: |
      {
        "text": "Build ${{ job.status }}",
        "blocks": [
          {
            "type": "section",
            "text": {
              "type": "mrkdwn",
              "text": "Build: ${{ job.status }}\nCommit: ${{ github.sha }}"
            }
          }
        ]
      }

docs.title: Upload Test Results
docs.text:
- name: Upload test results
  uses: dorny/test-reporter@v1
  if: always()
  with:
    name: Go Test Results
    path: '*.out'
    reporter: 'go-test'

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Quality Gate Pipeline Step
docs.text:
Goal: Enforce minimum quality before merge.

  1. Add pipeline steps for lint, unit tests, and coverage.
  2. Set a coverage threshold and fail when below threshold.
  3. Publish test and coverage reports as artifacts.
  4. Verify failure output is easy to diagnose.

Stretch: Add separate fast/slow test stages.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Safe Integration Test Stage
docs.text:
Goal: Keep CI reliable while running heavier tests.

  1. Add integration tests in a dedicated stage/job.
  2. Gate stage execution by branch or label rule.
  3. Capture logs and container output on failure.
  4. Ensure secrets are injected securely via CI variables.

Stretch: Add retry policy only for known flaky external dependency failures.

docs.title: Assignment: GitHub Actions Pipeline for Bookshelf
Goal
docs.text:
Create a production-ready CI workflow for the Bookshelf tutorial app.

This assignment transitions Bookshelf from local checks to enforceable pipeline governance.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add workflow file .github/workflows/ci.yml with jobs:
  2. lint
  3. unit
  4. integration

  5. contract

  6. Use Go setup + module caching in every job.

  7. Run commands:

go test -v -race ./...
go test -v ./tests/integration/...
go test -v ./tests/contract/consumer ./tests/contract/provider
  1. Upload artifacts from CI:
  2. coverage report

  3. pact files

  4. Ensure pull requests fail if any job fails.

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• A single push triggers all checks.
  • PR checks are visible and enforced before merge.

Also ensure failed jobs publish enough diagnostics for quick recovery.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Workflow Architecture for Scalable CI
Background
docs.text: As projects grow, CI reliability depends on job decomposition, caching discipline, and artifact strategy. Well-structured workflows are easier to debug and evolve.

docs.title: Architecture principles
docs.text:
1. Keep jobs single-purpose (lint, unit, integration, contract).
  2. Reuse setup steps consistently (Go version, cache keys, checkout depth).
  3. Upload diagnostics artifacts from failing jobs.
  4. Keep sensitive values in repository secrets only.

docs.title: Operational safeguards
docs.text:
• Use explicit timeouts per job.
  • Pin third-party action versions where possible.
  • Add branch protection so required checks gate merge.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Treat CI workflow files as production code: review changes carefully, test in PRs, and document why each gate exists.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Putting all checks into one monolithic job with poor failure isolation.
  • Using floating action versions without review strategy.
  • Inconsistent Go versions between jobs causing non-reproducible behavior.
  • Missing artifacts/logs for failed integration or contract jobs.

docs.title: Quick Workflow Checklist
docs.text:
• Are jobs split by responsibility (lint, unit, integration, contract)?
  • Are setup and cache patterns consistent across jobs?
  • Are action versions pinned and reviewed?
  • Are secrets scoped minimally and handled safely?
  • Are failure diagnostics uploaded automatically?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Running Unit and Integration Tests.

docs.title: Linting and Static Analysis
docs.text:
Linting and static analysis detect maintainability, correctness, and security risks before runtime tests execute. These checks are most effective when they are deterministic, high-signal, and consistently enforced in CI.

This section explains how to build a practical static quality gate for Go projects.

docs.title: Go Vet
docs.text:
Built-in static analysis tool:

go vet ./...

Use go vet as a baseline correctness gate in every pull request.

docs.title: Running in CI
docs.text:
- name: Run go vet
  run: go vet ./...

docs.title: golangci-lint
docs.text:
Combines multiple linters:

go install github.com/golangci/golangci-lint/cmd/golangci-lint@latest

golangci-lint run ./...

Pin the linter version in CI for reproducible results.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Create .golangci.yml:

linters:
  enable:
    - misspell
    - goimports
    - lll
    - unused
    - ineffassign
    - deadcode
    - unconvert
    - staticcheck

linters-settings:
  lll:
    line-length: 120

  goimports:
    local-prefixes: github.com/mycompany

output:
  format: colored-line-number

Tune enabled linters gradually to avoid overwhelming teams with low-value noise.

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
- uses: golangci/golangci-lint-action@v3
  with:
    version: latest
    args: --timeout=5m

docs.title: fmt
docs.text:
Format code:

go fmt ./...

# Check without modifying
gofmt -l ./...

Formatting checks should be mandatory and fail fast.

docs.title: goimports
docs.text:
Format imports:

go install golang.org/x/tools/cmd/goimports@latest

goimports -w ./...

docs.title: Cyclomatic Complexity
docs.text:
Complexity metrics help identify areas that are difficult to test and maintain.

Check code complexity with gocyclo:

go install github.com/fzipp/gocyclo/cmd/gocyclo@latest

gocyclo -over 10 ./...

docs.title: Error Checking
docs.text: Unchecked errors are a common source of latent production defects.

docs.title: errcheck
docs.text:
Ensures errors are handled:

go install github.com/kisielk/errcheck@latest

errcheck ./...

docs.title: nilcheck
docs.text:
Detect potential nil pointer dereferences:

go install golang.org/x/tools/go/analysis/passes/nilcheck@latest

docs.title: Security Scanning
docs.text: Security analyzers should be integrated as routine quality checks, not occasional audits.

docs.title: gosec
docs.text:
Security-focused analyzer:

go install github.com/securego/gosec/v2/cmd/gosec@latest

gosec ./...

docs.title: Trivy
docs.text:
Vulnerability scanner:

trivy fs .

docs.title: Complete CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text:
A complete quality pipeline should separate formatter, correctness, and security findings clearly.

name: Quality Checks

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  quality:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'

      - name: Format check
        run: |
          gofmt -l .
          if [ -n "$(gofmt -l .)" ]; then
            echo "Go files not formatted"
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Go vet
        run: go vet ./...

      - name: golangci-lint
        uses: golangci/golangci-lint-action@v3
        with:
          args: --timeout=5m

      - name: Complexity check
        run: |
          go install github.com/fzipp/gocyclo/cmd/gocyclo@latest
          gocyclo -over 10 ./...

      - name: Security check
        run: |
          go install github.com/securego/gosec/v2/cmd/gosec@latest
          gosec ./...

docs.title: Pre-commit Hook
docs.text:
Pre-commit hooks improve local feedback speed, while CI remains the source of truth.

Create .git/hooks/pre-commit:

#!/bin/bash

# Format check
if ! gofmt -l . | grep -q .; then
  echo "Please format your code with: go fmt ./..."
  exit 1
fi

# Run vet
if ! go vet ./...; then
  echo "Go vet failed"
  exit 1
fi

# Run tests
if ! go test -v ./...; then
  echo "Tests failed"
  exit 1
fi

exit 0

Make executable:

chmod +x .git/hooks/pre-commit

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Run linting on every commit
  • Fix issues before submitting PR
  • Enforce linting in CI
  • Keep linter configuration consistent
  • Address security warnings immediately
  • Monitor code quality metrics over time

Additional guidance:

  • Keep suppression lists explicit and reviewed.
  • Separate warning-only and blocking policies by severity.
  • Track recurring findings by category to guide refactoring priorities.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Quality Gate Pipeline Step
docs.text:
Goal: Enforce minimum quality before merge.

  1. Add pipeline steps for lint, unit tests, and coverage.
  2. Set a coverage threshold and fail when below threshold.
  3. Publish test and coverage reports as artifacts.
  4. Verify failure output is easy to diagnose.

Stretch: Add separate fast/slow test stages.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Safe Integration Test Stage
docs.text:
Goal: Keep CI reliable while running heavier tests.

  1. Add integration tests in a dedicated stage/job.
  2. Gate stage execution by branch or label rule.
  3. Capture logs and container output on failure.
  4. Ensure secrets are injected securely via CI variables.

Stretch: Add retry policy only for known flaky external dependency failures.

docs.title: Assignment: Lint and Static Analysis Gate for Bookshelf
Goal
docs.text:
Add mandatory static quality checks for Bookshelf code before merge.

This assignment ensures static analysis gates are enforceable and team-visible.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create .golangci.yml tuned for this repo.
  2. Add CI steps:
  3. go fmt ./...
  4. go vet ./...
  5. golangci-lint run ./...
  6. Add security scanning step:
gosec ./...
  1. Fail CI on formatting drift:
if [ -n "$(gofmt -l .)" ]; then
  echo "Unformatted files found"
  gofmt -l .
  exit 1
fi

docs.title: Bookshelf Focus Areas
docs.text:
• Ensure handler error paths always return JSON.
  • Ensure goroutine code in bulk import is race-safe.
  • Ensure context-aware repository methods handle errors.

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Lint + vet + security checks are blocking in CI.
  • Contributors cannot merge code with quality gate failures.

Also ensure failure messages include exact remediation hints.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Designing High-Signal Quality Gates
Background
docs.text: Static checks are most valuable when they are predictable and high-signal. If a gate is noisy, teams stop trusting it; if it is too weak, defects slip through.

docs.title: High-signal gate design
docs.text:
1. Keep formatter checks deterministic and mandatory.
  2. Separate style issues from correctness issues in job output.
  3. Run go vet and golangci-lint on every PR.
  4. Add security scanning as a distinct stage with clear failure messages.

docs.title: Suggested failure policy
docs.text:
• Block on formatting drift.
  • Block on vet/lint correctness findings.
  • Triage security findings by severity and track suppressions explicitly.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Use stable linter versions in CI to avoid surprise breakages from tool updates during active sprint work.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Enabling too many linters at once without triage strategy.
  • Ignoring formatter drift because other checks pass.
  • Treating security scanner output as informational only.
  • Allowing broad suppressions without review.

docs.title: Quick Static Gate Checklist
docs.text:
• Are format checks mandatory and deterministic?
  • Are vet and lint running on every PR?
  • Is linter version pinned for reproducibility?
  • Are security findings triaged with policy?
  • Are suppressions documented and reviewable?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Golden Files Pattern.

docs.title: Running Unit and Integration Tests
docs.text:
Execution strategy is a quality and productivity decision. Running the right tests at the right stage improves defect detection speed while keeping CI cost under control.

This guide defines practical command patterns for local development and CI parity.

docs.title: Test Execution
docs.text: Start with broad test commands, then use filters and tags to narrow scope for faster iteration.

docs.title: Basic Test Run
docs.text: go test ./...

docs.title: Verbose Output
docs.text: go test -v ./...

docs.title: Run Specific Test
docs.text: go test -run TestUserService ./...

docs.title: Run Tests Matching Pattern
docs.text: go test -run TestUser/GetUser ./...

docs.title: Test Filters
docs.text: Filtering helps isolate failures quickly during triage.

docs.title: Skip Certain Tests
docs.text: go test -skip TestSlowOperation ./...

docs.title: Run Only Unit Tests
docs.text:
go test -short ./...

Tests can use t.Skip() when testing.Short() is true.

docs.title: Timeout
docs.text:
go test -timeout 30s ./...

Always set explicit timeouts for long-running suites in CI.

docs.title: Race Detection
docs.text:
go test -race ./...

Find data races in concurrent code.

Use race detection regularly on unit suites and concurrency-heavy packages.

docs.title: Separate Unit and Integration Tests
docs.text: Separation ensures fast feedback for unit checks and controlled execution for heavier integration checks.

docs.title: Build Tags
docs.text:
// integration_test.go
//go:build integration

package myapp_test

func TestDatabaseIntegration(t *testing.T) {
    // Integration test
}

// unit_test.go
//go:build !integration

package myapp_test

func TestUserService(t *testing.T) {
    // Unit test
}

Run unit tests only:

go test ./...

Run integration tests:

go test -tags=integration ./...

docs.title: Parallel Execution
docs.text:
Parallelism speeds execution, but only for tests that are data-isolated and deterministic.

go test -parallel 4 ./...

Tests must be marked with t.Parallel():

func TestConcurrent(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()
    // Test code
}

docs.title: Coverage Analysis
docs.text:
Combine coverage with execution groups to identify gaps per test layer.

go test -cover ./...
go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
go tool cover -html=coverage.out

docs.title: Test Output Formats
docs.text: Structured output helps CI tools parse and surface failures effectively.

docs.title: JSON Output
docs.text: go test -json ./...

docs.title: Verbose with Timing
docs.text: go test -v -timeout 5m ./...

docs.title: CI/CD Integration
docs.text: Split unit and integration jobs to improve observability and rerun efficiency.

docs.title: GitHub Actions: Unit + Integration
docs.text:
name: Tests

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  unit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
        with:
          go-version: '1.21'
      - run: go test -v -race ./...
