  integration:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v4
      - run: go test -v -tags=integration -timeout=20m ./...

docs.title: Makefile Example
docs.text:
test:
    go test -v -race ./...

test-unit:
    go test -v -short ./...

test-integration:
    go test -v -tags=integration -timeout=20m ./...

test-all: test-unit test-integration

test-coverage:
    go test -v -coverprofile=coverage.out ./...
    go tool cover -html=coverage.out

Keep Makefile targets aligned with CI commands to avoid local/CI drift.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Run unit tests before integration tests
  • Use t.Parallel() for independent tests
  • Use race detector in CI
  • Set appropriate timeouts
  • Separate concerns with build tags
  • Monitor test execution time

Additional guidance:

  • Keep integration test setup deterministic and isolated.
  • Fail fast on unit regressions before running expensive suites.
  • Publish timing trends to detect gradual slowdowns.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Quality Gate Pipeline Step
docs.text:
Goal: Enforce minimum quality before merge.

  1. Add pipeline steps for lint, unit tests, and coverage.
  2. Set a coverage threshold and fail when below threshold.
  3. Publish test and coverage reports as artifacts.
  4. Verify failure output is easy to diagnose.

Stretch: Add separate fast/slow test stages.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Safe Integration Test Stage
docs.text:
Goal: Keep CI reliable while running heavier tests.

  1. Add integration tests in a dedicated stage/job.
  2. Gate stage execution by branch or label rule.
  3. Capture logs and container output on failure.
  4. Ensure secrets are injected securely via CI variables.

Stretch: Add retry policy only for known flaky external dependency failures.

docs.title: Assignment: Test Execution Strategy for Bookshelf
Goal
docs.text:
Define repeatable commands for local and CI execution of all Bookshelf tests.

This assignment standardizes how contributors run and reason about test layers.

docs.title: Local Commands
docs.text:
# Fast feedback (domain + handler unit tests)
go test -v -short ./...

# Full unit pass with race detector
go test -v -race ./...

# Integration only
go test -v -timeout=20m ./tests/integration/...

# Contract only
go test -v ./tests/contract/consumer ./tests/contract/provider

docs.title: Makefile Targets
docs.text:
Add or update targets:

test-unit:
  go test -v -race ./...

test-integration:
  go test -v -timeout=20m ./tests/integration/...

test-contract:
  go test -v ./tests/contract/consumer ./tests/contract/provider

test-all: test-unit test-integration test-contract

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• make test-all runs complete validation suite.
  • CI uses the same command set to avoid drift.

Also verify command docs are visible to new contributors in onboarding paths.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Test Pyramid Execution Strategy
Background
docs.text: Execution order determines feedback quality and CI cost. Fast failures should happen early, expensive suites should run after quick confidence gates.

docs.title: Recommended sequence
docs.text:
1. Unit tests with race detector.
  2. Integration tests with bounded timeout.
  3. Contract tests for consumer/provider compatibility.

docs.title: Pipeline behavior goals
docs.text:
• Fail fast on unit-level regressions.
  • Keep integration runs isolated and deterministic.
  • Ensure contract checks run before deployment logic.

docs.title: Local parity guidance
docs.text: Local commands should mirror CI command groups. This reduces "works on my machine" failures and shortens PR turnaround.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Publish timing data per test group to identify slowdowns and keep feedback loops tight.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Running all suites with one command for every local iteration.
  • Mixing integration and unit responsibilities in same test package without tags.
  • Missing timeouts for long-running integration jobs.
  • Enabling parallel test execution without state isolation.

docs.title: Quick Test Execution Checklist
docs.text:
• Are unit and integration commands clearly separated?
  • Are race checks part of regular validation?
  • Are integration and contract suites time-bounded?
  • Do local commands mirror CI behavior?
  • Are failures easy to classify by test layer?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Coverage and Thresholds.

docs.title: Debugging Concurrent Tests
docs.text:
Concurrent test debugging is about making nondeterministic behavior observable and controllable. Races, deadlocks, and goroutine leaks often hide until load or timing changes expose them.

This guide focuses on practical diagnostics that convert intermittent concurrency failures into repeatable findings.

docs.title: Challenges with Concurrent Tests
docs.text: Concurrency failures usually involve ordering assumptions that are not encoded explicitly in tests.

docs.title: Race Conditions
docs.text:
Data races occur when multiple goroutines access the same memory without synchronization.

# Detect races
go test -race ./...

docs.title: Deadlocks
docs.text: Goroutines waiting indefinitely for resources.

docs.title: Timeout Issues
docs.text:
Tests take longer with concurrency.

Long runtimes are often a symptom of coordination bugs, not just slow code.

docs.title: Race Detection
docs.text: Race detection should be part of regular local and CI runs for concurrency-heavy packages.

docs.title: Enable Race Detector
docs.text:
go test -race ./...
go run -race main.go

docs.title: Example Race Condition
docs.text:
// BAD - Data race
var counter int

func TestRace(t *testing.T) {
    go func() { counter++ }()
    go func() { counter++ }()

    time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
    if counter != 2 {
        t.Errorf("counter = %d; want 2", counter)
    }
}

// GOOD - Synchronized
var counter int
var mu sync.Mutex

func TestRaceSafe(t *testing.T) {
    go func() {
        mu.Lock()
        counter++
        mu.Unlock()
    }()
    go func() {
        mu.Lock()
        counter++
        mu.Unlock()
    }()

    time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
    if counter != 2 {
        t.Errorf("counter = %d; want 2", counter)
    }
}

The key fix is synchronization around shared mutable state and deterministic completion signaling.

docs.title: Debugging Goroutine Issues
docs.text: Goroutine lifecycle checks are critical for finding leaks and blocked workers.

docs.title: Goroutine Leaks
docs.text:
import "runtime"

func TestNoGoroutineLeaks(t *testing.T) {
    before := runtime.NumGoroutine()

    // Test code with goroutines
    doAsyncWork()

    // Give goroutines time to finish
    time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)

    after := runtime.NumGoroutine()

    if after > before {
        t.Errorf("goroutine leak: before=%d, after=%d", before, after)
    }
}

docs.title: Print Stack Traces
docs.text:
import "runtime/debug"

func TestWithStackTrace(t *testing.T) {
    defer func() {
        if r := recover(); r != nil {
            debug.PrintStack()
            t.Fatalf("panic: %v", r)
        }
    }()

    // Test code
}

Stack traces are especially useful when test failures only occur in CI timing conditions.

docs.title: Debugging Channels
docs.text: Channel bugs are often coordination bugs. Validate sender/receiver ownership explicitly.

docs.title: Unbuffered Channel Deadlock
docs.text:
// BAD - Deadlock
func TestChannelDeadlock(t *testing.T) {
    ch := make(chan int)  // Unbuffered

    ch <- 1  // Blocks - no receiver
    val := <-ch  // Never reached

    if val != 1 {
        t.Error("wrong value")
    }
}

// GOOD - With goroutine
func TestChannelSafe(t *testing.T) {
    ch := make(chan int)

    go func() {
        ch <- 1
    }()

    val := <-ch
    if val != 1 {
        t.Error("wrong value")
    }
}

docs.title: Timeout Handling
docs.text: Timeouts should be explicit and tied to expected operation boundaries.

docs.title: Set Test Timeout
docs.text: go test -timeout 30s ./...

docs.title: Per-Test Timeout with Context
docs.text:
func TestWithTimeout(t *testing.T) {
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
    defer cancel()

    result := make(chan string)

    go func() {
        time.Sleep(2 * time.Second)
        result <- "done"
    }()

    select {
    case <-ctx.Done():
        t.Fatalf("timeout: %v", ctx.Err())
    case res := <-result:
        if res != "done" {
            t.Errorf("unexpected result: %s", res)
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Testing Concurrent Operations
docs.text: Use scenario-based concurrency ranges to validate behavior under realistic worker counts.

docs.title: Table-Driven Concurrent Test
docs.text:
func TestConcurrentOperations(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        name      string
        workers   int
        operations int
    }{
        {"single worker", 1, 10},
        {"multiple workers", 5, 10},
        {"high concurrency", 100, 100},
    }

    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            var wg sync.WaitGroup
            results := make(chan int, tt.workers)

            for i := 0; i < tt.workers; i++ {
                wg.Add(1)
                go func(id int) {
                    defer wg.Done()
                    for j := 0; j < tt.operations; j++ {
                        results <- id*tt.operations + j
                    }
                }(i)
            }

            wg.Wait()
            close(results)

            count := len(results)
            expected := tt.workers * tt.operations

            if count != expected {
                t.Errorf("processed %d items; want %d", count, expected)
            }
        })
    }
}

docs.title: Debugging Tool: pprof
docs.text: Profiling helps distinguish CPU contention from blocked concurrency paths.

docs.title: Generate CPU Profile
docs.text:
import _ "net/http/pprof"

func TestWithProfile(t *testing.T) {
    f, _ := os.Create("cpu.prof")
    pprof.StartCPUProfile(f)
    defer pprof.StopCPUProfile()

    // Test code

    // View: go tool pprof cpu.prof
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Run tests with -race flag always
  • Use sync.WaitGroup for coordination
  • Use channels for communication
  • Set reasonable timeouts
  • Avoid busy-wait loops
  • Use select with timeout
  • Test concurrent code multiple times
  • Keep concurrent tests focused

Additional guidance:

  • Prefer explicit completion channels over fixed sleeps.
  • Keep shared state ownership clear by design.
  • Add repeated-run commands for intermittent failure detection.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Reproduce and Stabilize a Flaky Test
docs.text:
Goal: Practice a repeatable flake-debugging workflow.

  1. Select one flaky test scenario.
  2. Run it repeatedly and capture failure evidence.
  3. Identify root cause (timing, shared state, ordering, etc.).
  4. Apply one stabilization fix and re-run to confirm.

Stretch: Add a regression test that would have caught the original flake.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Add a Reusable Test Helper Pattern
docs.text:
Goal: Improve readability and reduce duplication.

  1. Identify repeated setup/assertion code in tests.
  2. Extract a helper (builder, fixture, or assertion helper).
  3. Refactor at least two tests to use it.
  4. Verify tests remain clear and deterministic.

Stretch: Document when to use and avoid this helper.

docs.title: Assignment: Debug Bookshelf Bulk Import Concurrency
Goal
docs.text:
Harden concurrent tests around BulkImportService and eliminate race/deadlock risks.

This assignment creates a durable confidence layer for Bookshelf concurrency behavior.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Add pkg/domain/bulk_import_concurrency_test.go.
  2. Stress test parallel import with multiple worker counts.
  3. Add timeout-safe test using context and select.
  4. Add goroutine leak check around import calls.

Run commands:

go test -race -count=20 ./pkg/domain -run TestBulkImport
go test -v -timeout=30s ./pkg/domain -run TestBulkImport

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• No race detector failures.
  • No deadlock/timeout under repeated runs.
  • Tests are deterministic across 20 runs.

Also ensure failures include enough context (worker count, operation count, timeout path) for quick triage.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Concurrency Test Reliability Model
Background
docs.text: Concurrent tests fail in non-obvious ways: races, deadlocks, starvation, and leak accumulation. Robust tests coordinate explicitly and observe lifecycle boundaries.

docs.title: Reliability checklist
docs.text:
1. Use WaitGroup or channel completion signaling for goroutine lifecycle.
  2. Guard shared mutable state with mutexes or channel ownership.
  3. Use context and select for cancellation-safe waits.
  4. Include test-level timeout boundaries.

docs.title: Failure forensics hints
docs.text:
• Race detector output usually points to real shared-state design flaws.
  • Hanging tests usually indicate missing receiver/sender coordination.
  • Goroutine growth over repeated runs suggests leak or blocked workers.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Run critical concurrency suites with both -race and repeated execution in CI to detect intermittent hazards early.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Using fixed time.Sleep as synchronization logic.
  • Sharing mutable state without ownership or locking policy.
  • Running high-parallel tests without timeout boundaries.
  • Ignoring goroutine count growth between repeated test runs.

docs.title: Quick Concurrency Debug Checklist
docs.text:
• Is shared state synchronized or ownership-based?
  • Are goroutine lifecycles explicitly awaited?
  • Are channel send/receive paths guaranteed to complete?
  • Are timeouts and cancellation paths covered?
  • Do repeated -race runs stay stable?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Playground Overview.

docs.title: Flaky Test Diagnostics
docs.text:
Flaky tests are reliability debt: they slow delivery, reduce trust in CI, and hide real regressions. Effective flake handling requires a repeatable diagnostic process, not ad hoc reruns.

This guide explains how to detect, classify, and permanently fix flaky tests.

docs.title: What are Flaky Tests?
docs.text:
Tests that pass inconsistently - sometimes pass, sometimes fail, without code changes.

Treat flakiness as a product quality issue, not just a test inconvenience.

docs.title: Concurrency Issues
docs.text:
• Race conditions
  • Unordered map iteration
  • Goroutine timing issues

docs.title: External Dependencies
docs.text:
• Network timeouts
  • Database connection issues
  • Service availability

docs.title: Timing Issues
docs.text:
• Insufficient wait time
  • Race conditions with time
  • Sleep durations too short

docs.title: Resource Issues
docs.text:
• Insufficient file handles
  • Memory limitations
  • Port conflicts

docs.title: Detection Strategies
docs.text: Start with reproducible repeated execution, then layer race and parallel stress diagnostics.

docs.title: Repeated Execution
docs.text:
# Run test 10 times
for i in {1..10}; do
    go test -run TestFlaky ./...
done

docs.title: With Race Detector
docs.text: go test -race -count=10 ./...

docs.title: Stress Testing
docs.text: go test -run TestFlaky -count=100 -parallel=4 ./...

docs.title: Diagnosis Tools
docs.text: Use diagnostics to classify root cause before changing test logic.

docs.title: pprof for Goroutine Issues
docs.text:
import _ "net/http/pprof"

func TestGoroutineLeaks(t *testing.T) {
    before := runtime.NumGoroutine()

    // Test code

    runtime.GC()
    after := runtime.NumGoroutine()

    if after > before+1 {
        t.Errorf("goroutine leak detected: %d -> %d", before, after)
    }
}

docs.title: Timeout Issues
docs.text:
func TestWithTimeout(t *testing.T) {
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
    defer cancel()

    // Test with context timeout
    result := someAsyncOperation(ctx)
    if result == nil {
        t.Error("operation timed out")
    }
}

Timeout-based diagnosis helps separate slow infrastructure from coordination bugs.

docs.title: Fixing Flaky Tests
docs.text: Prioritize deterministic synchronization and explicit test seams.

docs.title: Use testify/require for Assertions
docs.text:
import "github.com/stretchr/testify/require"

func TestFlaky(t *testing.T) {
    result := getSomeValue()
    require.NotNil(t, result)  // Stops immediately on failure
}

docs.title: Proper Waiting
docs.text:
// Bad - fixed sleep
time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)

// Good - wait for condition
require.Eventually(t, func() bool {
    return service.IsReady()
}, 5*time.Second, 100*time.Millisecond)

docs.title: Use testify/assert for Complex Conditions
docs.text:
import "github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"

func TestOrderProcessing(t *testing.T) {
    order := processOrder()

    assert.Equal(t, "completed", order.Status)
    assert.Equal(t, 99.99, order.Total)
    assert.NotNil(t, order.ConfirmedAt)
}

docs.title: Debugging Flaky Tests
docs.text: Add temporary high-signal logs and remove them once root cause is confirmed.

docs.title: Add Logging
docs.text:
func TestWithLogging(t *testing.T) {
    t.Logf("Starting test at %v", time.Now())

    result, err := someOperation()

    t.Logf("Operation completed with result: %v, error: %v", result, err)

    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("operation failed: %v", err)
    }
}

docs.title: Capture Goroutine Stack Traces
docs.text:
import "runtime/debug"

func TestGoroutineStack(t *testing.T) {
    defer func() {
        if t.Failed() {
            debug.PrintStack()
        }
    }()

    // Test code
}

docs.title: Example: Fixing a Flaky Database Test
docs.text: This example shows the core pattern: replace fixed timing assumptions with condition-based waits.

docs.title: Before (Flaky)
docs.text:
func TestUserCreation(t *testing.T) {
    user := createUser("John")

    // Race condition - query might run before write completes
    retrieved, err := getUser(user.ID)
    require.NoError(t, err)
    require.Equal(t, "John", retrieved.Name)
}

docs.title: After (Fixed)
docs.text:
func TestUserCreation(t *testing.T) {
    user := createUser("John")

    // Wait for user to be retrievable
    require.Eventually(t, func() bool {
        retrieved, err := getUser(user.ID)
        if err != nil {
            return false
        }
        return retrieved.Name == "John"
    }, 2*time.Second, 100*time.Millisecond)
}

docs.title: Preventing Flaky Tests
docs.text: Prevention is cheaper than triage. Favor architecture that makes time, randomness, and dependencies controllable in tests.

docs.title: Use Dependency Injection
docs.text:
type Service struct {
    clock Clock  // Mockable clock
}

func NewService(clock Clock) *Service {
    return &Service{clock: clock}
}

docs.title: Control Time in Tests
docs.text:
type MockClock struct {
    now time.Time
}

func (m *MockClock) Now() time.Time {
    return m.now
}

func (m *MockClock) Sleep(d time.Duration) {
    m.now = m.now.Add(d)
}

docs.title: Isolate External Dependencies
docs.text:
func TestWithMockedAPI(t *testing.T) {
    server := httptest.NewServer(http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
        w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
        fmt.Fprint(w, `{"id":1,"name":"John"}`)
    }))
    defer server.Close()

    client := NewAPIClient(server.URL)
    user, err := client.GetUser(1)

    require.NoError(t, err)
    require.Equal(t, "John", user.Name)
}

docs.title: Monitoring in CI
docs.text:
Periodic repeated-run checks are useful for high-risk suites and newly stabilized tests.

- name: Detect flaky tests
  run: |
    for i in {1..3}; do
      echo "Run $i"
      go test -v -count=10 ./... || exit 1
    done

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Reproduce and Stabilize a Flaky Test
docs.text:
Goal: Practice a repeatable flake-debugging workflow.

  1. Select one flaky test scenario.
  2. Run it repeatedly and capture failure evidence.
  3. Identify root cause (timing, shared state, ordering, etc.).
  4. Apply one stabilization fix and re-run to confirm.

Stretch: Add a regression test that would have caught the original flake.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Add a Reusable Test Helper Pattern
docs.text:
Goal: Improve readability and reduce duplication.

  1. Identify repeated setup/assertion code in tests.
  2. Extract a helper (builder, fixture, or assertion helper).
  3. Refactor at least two tests to use it.
  4. Verify tests remain clear and deterministic.

Stretch: Document when to use and avoid this helper.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text:
Identify and fix flaky tests across handler, integration, and contract layers.

This assignment introduces a practical stabilization workflow for the Bookshelf test suite.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Run flaky detector locally:
go test -race -count=30 ./pkg/handler ./pkg/domain
go test -count=10 ./tests/integration/...
  1. Replace fixed time.Sleep(...) usage with eventual checks (require.Eventually or retry loops).
  2. Ensure tests do not share mutable global state.
  3. Add richer diagnostics:
    • t.Log timestamps
    • request/response payload on failure
    • container logs in integration failures

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Previously flaky tests pass repeatedly across 30 local runs.
  • CI can run repeated mode for flaky-prone suites.

Also ensure every fixed flake has a documented root cause category.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Flaky failures erode confidence in CI and slow delivery. A repeatable triage workflow reduces guesswork and helps teams fix root causes instead of suppressing symptoms.

docs.title: Triage sequence
docs.text:
1. Reproduce with repetition (-count, -race).
  2. Classify failure type: timing, shared state, external dependency, ordering.
  3. Add temporary diagnostics to isolate state transitions.
  4. Replace nondeterministic behavior with controllable test seams.

docs.title: High-value remediation actions
docs.text:
• Remove fixed sleeps and wait for explicit conditions.
  • Isolate tests from shared global state.
  • Make time, randomness, and external calls injectable.
  • Capture failure artifacts (logs, payloads, container output).

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Track top flaky tests in a team list and prioritize by failure frequency multiplied by pipeline impact.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Re-running failing tests until green without root-cause analysis.
  • Masking nondeterminism with larger fixed sleeps.
  • Leaving shared mutable fixtures across tests.
  • Disabling flaky tests indefinitely without stabilization plan.

docs.title: Quick Flake Triage Checklist
docs.text:
• Can failure be reproduced with -count and -race?
  • Is root cause category identified (timing/state/dependency/order)?
  • Were fixed sleeps replaced with explicit condition waits?
  • Are external dependencies isolated or controlled?
  • Is the stabilization verified with repeated CI-like runs?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Debugging Concurrent Tests.

docs.title: Golden Files Pattern
docs.text:
Golden files are a contract-preservation technique for complex outputs. They provide a fast way to detect meaningful output drift while keeping expected behavior visible in version control.

This section focuses on sustainable snapshot testing practices that avoid brittle or noisy comparisons.

docs.title: What are Golden Files?
docs.text:
Golden files are reference outputs used to validate that the application produces the expected results. Also called "snapshot testing" or "approval testing".

They are most effective when output is normalized and review discipline is strong.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• API response validation
  • Report generation
  • Configuration output
  • SQL query results
  • File content validation

Use golden tests when output shape and content are more important than internal implementation.

docs.title: Basic Pattern
docs.text: The basic lifecycle is: generate output, normalize unstable fields, compare with approved baseline, and review intentional updates.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
package myapp_test

import (
    "os"
    "path/filepath"
    "testing"
)

const goldenDir = "testdata/golden"

func loadGoldenFile(t *testing.T, name string) string {
    path := filepath.Join(goldenDir, name)
    data, err := os.ReadFile(path)
    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("failed to read golden file %s: %v", path, err)
    }
    return string(data)
}

func saveGoldenFile(t *testing.T, name, content string) {
    path := filepath.Join(goldenDir, name)
    err := os.WriteFile(path, []byte(content), 0644)
    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("failed to write golden file %s: %v", path, err)
    }
}

docs.title: Test Example
docs.text:
func TestGenerateReport(t *testing.T) {
    update := os.Getenv("UPDATE_GOLDEN") != ""

    report := GenerateReport()

    if update {
        saveGoldenFile(t, "report.txt", report)
        t.Skip("Skipping test, golden file updated")
    }

    expected := loadGoldenFile(t, "report.txt")

    if report != expected {
        t.Errorf("report does not match golden file")
        t.Logf("Got:\n%s", report)
        t.Logf("Expected:\n%s", expected)
    }
}

Explicit update mode prevents accidental baseline rewrites during normal test runs.

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
# Normal run - compare against golden files
go test ./...

# Update golden files
UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 go test ./...

docs.title: JSON Golden Files
docs.text: JSON snapshots are especially useful for API contract and handler response stability.

docs.title: API Response Testing
docs.text:
func TestUserAPIResponse(t *testing.T) {
    update := os.Getenv("UPDATE_GOLDEN") != ""

    resp, err := getUserAPI(1)
    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("unexpected error: %v", err)
    }

    // Normalize response (remove timestamps, IDs that vary)
    normalized := NormalizeResponse(resp)

    data, err := json.MarshalIndent(normalized, "", "  ")
    if err != nil {
        t.Fatalf("marshal error: %v", err)
    }

    if update {
        saveGoldenFile(t, "user_response.json", string(data))
        t.Skip("Skipping test, golden file updated")
    }

    expected := loadGoldenFile(t, "user_response.json")

    if string(data) != expected {
        t.Errorf("response does not match golden file")
    }
}

docs.title: Diffing Tool Integration
docs.text: Human-readable diff output reduces triage time when snapshots change.

docs.title: With testdata
docs.text:
import "github.com/google/go-cmp/cmp"

func TestWithDiff(t *testing.T) {
    result := ProcessData()
    expected := loadGoldenFile(t, "output.txt")

    if diff := cmp.Diff(expected, result); diff != "" {
        t.Errorf("output mismatch (-want +got):\n%s", diff)
    }
}

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
myapp/
├── myapp.go
├── myapp_test.go
└── testdata/
    └── golden/
        ├── report.txt
        ├── user_response.json
        └── config_output.yaml

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: Golden file reliability depends on normalization and review quality.

docs.title: Normalize Dates and IDs
docs.text:
func NormalizeResponse(resp *Response) *Response {
    // Replace timestamps with fixed value
    resp.CreatedAt = "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z"

    // Replace generated IDs
    resp.ID = "generated-id"

    return resp
}

docs.title: Version Golden Files
docs.text:
testdata/
└── golden/
    ├── v1/
    │   └── response.json
    └── v2/
        └── response.json

docs.title: Review Changes
docs.text:
# Show what changed
git diff testdata/golden/

Require reviewers to classify each snapshot change as intended behavior, format noise, or regression.

docs.title: Pros and Cons
docs.text: Use this section to decide where snapshots are high value versus where targeted assertions are clearer.

docs.title: Pros
docs.text:
• Catch unintended changes
  • Good for complex outputs
  • Self-documenting tests
  • Fast feedback

docs.title: Cons
docs.text:
• Need review process
  • Can mask real issues
  • Large diffs hard to review
  • Need normalization

docs.title: Integration with CI
docs.text:
CI should detect unexpected golden updates and force explicit review.

- name: Check golden files updated
  run: |
    git status testdata/golden/ | grep modified
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
      echo "Golden files were modified - review changes"
      git diff testdata/golden/
      exit 1
    fi

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Reproduce and Stabilize a Flaky Test
docs.text:
Goal: Practice a repeatable flake-debugging workflow.

  1. Select one flaky test scenario.
  2. Run it repeatedly and capture failure evidence.
  3. Identify root cause (timing, shared state, ordering, etc.).
  4. Apply one stabilization fix and re-run to confirm.

Stretch: Add a regression test that would have caught the original flake.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Add a Reusable Test Helper Pattern
docs.text:
Goal: Improve readability and reduce duplication.

  1. Identify repeated setup/assertion code in tests.
  2. Extract a helper (builder, fixture, or assertion helper).
  3. Refactor at least two tests to use it.
  4. Verify tests remain clear and deterministic.

Stretch: Document when to use and avoid this helper.

docs.title: Assignment: Golden Files for Bookshelf API Responses
Goal
docs.text:
Use golden files to lock down stable JSON responses from Bookshelf handlers.

This assignment creates a maintainable response-regression safety net for Bookshelf APIs.

docs.title: Route Prefix Note
docs.text: Snapshot whichever route prefix your implementation currently exposes (/api in earlier sections or /api/v1 in capstone).

docs.title: Targets
docs.text:
1. GET /api/books response snapshot.
  2. POST /api/users success response snapshot.
  3. Validation error response snapshot.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create folder tests/testdata/golden/.
  2. Add handler tests in pkg/handler/handlers_golden_test.go.
  3. Normalize dynamic fields (id, timestamps) before compare.
  4. Add update mode with env var:
UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 go test ./pkg/handler -run Golden

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Golden tests pass in normal mode.
  • Golden files only change intentionally and are reviewed in PR.

Also ensure update instructions are documented for contributors.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Golden files are powerful for complex outputs, but become noisy if dynamic fields are not normalized or review discipline is weak.

docs.title: Sustainability rules
docs.text:
1. Normalize unstable fields (IDs, timestamps, nondeterministic ordering).
  2. Keep snapshots focused on meaningful output contracts.
  3. Require PR review for every golden update.
  4. Keep update mode explicit (UPDATE_GOLDEN=1).

docs.title: Review heuristics
docs.text:
• Ask whether snapshot changes are expected product behavior.
  • Check for accidental formatting-only churn.
  • Confirm no sensitive data was written to golden files.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Use golden tests for high-value response schemas and generated artifacts, not as a replacement for targeted behavioral assertions.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Snapshotting unstable fields without normalization.
  • Updating golden files in bulk without intent review.
  • Using golden snapshots for trivial outputs better asserted directly.
  • Allowing formatting-only churn to dominate review signal.

docs.title: Quick Golden Review Checklist
docs.text:
• Are dynamic fields normalized before compare?
  • Is update mode explicit and deliberate?
  • Do diffs reflect meaningful behavior changes?
  • Are sensitive values excluded from snapshots?
  • Is each golden update reviewed in PR context?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Test Data Builders.

docs.title: Test Data Builders
docs.text:
Test data builders improve readability and maintainability by separating scenario intent from object-construction noise. They are especially valuable when domain models become larger and tests require many variations.

This guide explains how to design builders that remain predictable, reusable, and easy to evolve.

docs.title: Builder Pattern for Tests
docs.text:
The builder pattern makes it easy to create test data with flexible configuration.

A good builder makes the common case simple and edge cases explicit.

docs.title: Basic Builder
docs.text:
Start with valid defaults so tests only customize fields relevant to the behavior under test.

package myapp

// User domain model
type User struct {
    ID    int
    Name  string
    Email string
    Role  string
    Age   int
}

// UserBuilder for tests
type UserBuilder struct {
    user User
}

func NewUserBuilder() *UserBuilder {
    return &UserBuilder{
        user: User{
            ID:    1,
            Name:  "John",
            Email: "john@example.com",
            Role:  "user",
            Age:   30,
        },
    }
}

func (b *UserBuilder) WithID(id int) *UserBuilder {
    b.user.ID = id
    return b
}

func (b *UserBuilder) WithName(name string) *UserBuilder {
    b.user.Name = name
    return b
}

func (b *UserBuilder) WithEmail(email string) *UserBuilder {
    b.user.Email = email
    return b
}

func (b *UserBuilder) WithRole(role string) *UserBuilder {
    b.user.Role = role
    return b
}

func (b *UserBuilder) Build() User {
    return b.user
}

docs.title: Using the Builder
docs.text:
Builder usage should highlight scenario differences, not construction mechanics.

func TestUserService(t *testing.T) {
    // Simple case with defaults
    user1 := NewUserBuilder().Build()

    // Customize specific fields
    user2 := NewUserBuilder().
        WithName("Jane").
        WithEmail("jane@example.com").
        Build()

    // Build multiple variations
    users := []User{
        NewUserBuilder().WithRole("admin").Build(),
        NewUserBuilder().WithRole("user").Build(),
        NewUserBuilder().WithRole("guest").Build(),
    }

    // Use in test
    service := NewUserService()
    for _, user := range users {
        result := service.ProcessUser(user)
        // Assert result
    }
}

docs.title: Builder with Complex Objects
docs.text:
For object graphs, builders should avoid hidden side effects and keep defaults coherent.

type Order struct {
    ID        int
    UserID    int
    Items     []OrderItem
    Total     float64
    Status    string
    CreatedAt time.Time
}

type OrderItem struct {
    ProductID int
    Quantity  int
    Price     float64
}

type OrderBuilder struct {
    order Order
}

func NewOrderBuilder() *OrderBuilder {
    return &OrderBuilder{
        order: Order{
            ID:        1,
            UserID:    1,
            Items:     []OrderItem{},
            Total:     99.99,
            Status:    "pending",
            CreatedAt: time.Now(),
        },
    }
}

func (b *OrderBuilder) WithItem(productID, quantity int, price float64) *OrderBuilder {
    b.order.Items = append(b.order.Items, OrderItem{
        ProductID: productID,
        Quantity:  quantity,
        Price:     price,
    })
    return b
}

func (b *OrderBuilder) WithStatus(status string) *OrderBuilder {
    b.order.Status = status
    return b
}

func (b *OrderBuilder) Build() Order {
    return b.order
}

docs.title: Using Complex Builder
docs.text:
Complex builders are most useful when they encapsulate repetitive setup while preserving test intent.

func TestOrderProcessing(t *testing.T) {
    order := NewOrderBuilder().
        WithItem(1, 2, 25.00).
        WithItem(2, 1, 49.99).
        WithStatus("confirmed").
        Build()

    // Total will be recalculated
    total := order.CalculateTotal()

    if total != 99.99 {
        t.Errorf("expected 99.99, got %f", total)
    }
}

docs.title: Factory Builders
docs.text: Factory builders provide named presets for business-relevant test personas.

docs.title: Preset Scenarios
docs.text:
// Builders for common test scenarios
func AdminUserBuilder() *UserBuilder {
    return NewUserBuilder().
        WithRole("admin").
        WithEmail("admin@example.com")
}

func PremiumUserBuilder() *UserBuilder {
    return NewUserBuilder().
        WithRole("premium").
        WithName("Premium User")
}

// In tests
func TestAdminPermissions(t *testing.T) {
    user := AdminUserBuilder().Build()

    hasPermission := user.HasPermission("create_user")
    if !hasPermission {
        t.Error("admin should have create_user permission")
    }
}

docs.title: Builder with Sequences
docs.text:
Sequence builders are useful for list-oriented scenarios and deterministic fixture sets.

type SequenceBuilder struct {
    users []User
}

func NewSequenceBuilder() *SequenceBuilder {
    return &SequenceBuilder{users: []User{}}
}

func (b *SequenceBuilder) AddUser(name, email string) *SequenceBuilder {
    user := NewUserBuilder().
        WithID(len(b.users) + 1).
        WithName(name).
        WithEmail(email).
        Build()
    b.users = append(b.users, user)
    return b
}

func (b *SequenceBuilder) Build() []User {
    return b.users
}

// Usage
users := NewSequenceBuilder().
    AddUser("Alice", "alice@example.com").
    AddUser("Bob", "bob@example.com").
    AddUser("Charlie", "charlie@example.com").
    Build()

docs.title: Testing with Builders
docs.text:
Builder-driven test tables keep setup concise and assertions focused.

func TestUserRepository(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        name     string
        builder  func() User
        validate func(*testing.T, User)
    }{
        {
            name: "create admin user",
            builder: func() User {
                return AdminUserBuilder().Build()
            },
            validate: func(t *testing.T, u User) {
                if u.Role != "admin" {
                    t.Errorf("expected admin role")
                }
            },
        },
        {
            name: "create standard user",
            builder: func() User {
                return NewUserBuilder().Build()
            },
            validate: func(t *testing.T, u User) {
                if u.Role != "user" {
                    t.Errorf("expected user role")
                }
            },
        },
    }

    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            user := tt.builder()
            tt.validate(t, user)
        })
    }
}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Provide sensible defaults
  • Make builders chainable
  • Keep builders close to tests
  • Create factory methods for common scenarios
  • Don't over-engineer builders
  • Use for complex object graphs

Additional guidance:

  • Keep builders in test-only packages.
  • Validate defaults periodically as domain rules evolve.
  • Avoid embedding randomness in builder defaults.

docs.title: Quick Exercises (SDET Focus)
docs.text: Try these exercises before moving to the next section.

docs.title: Exercise 1: Reproduce and Stabilize a Flaky Test
docs.text:
Goal: Practice a repeatable flake-debugging workflow.

  1. Select one flaky test scenario.
  2. Run it repeatedly and capture failure evidence.
  3. Identify root cause (timing, shared state, ordering, etc.).
  4. Apply one stabilization fix and re-run to confirm.

Stretch: Add a regression test that would have caught the original flake.

docs.title: Exercise 2: Add a Reusable Test Helper Pattern
docs.text:
Goal: Improve readability and reduce duplication.

  1. Identify repeated setup/assertion code in tests.
  2. Extract a helper (builder, fixture, or assertion helper).
  3. Refactor at least two tests to use it.
  4. Verify tests remain clear and deterministic.

Stretch: Document when to use and avoid this helper.

docs.title: Assignment: Builders for Bookshelf Test Data
Goal
docs.text:
Create reusable builders for Users, Books, Reviews, and Shelf Entries to simplify tests.

This assignment standardizes fixture construction across Bookshelf tests.

docs.title: Tasks
docs.text:
1. Create tests/fixtures/builders.go with:
    • UserBuilder
    • BookBuilder
    • ReviewBuilder
    • ShelfEntryBuilder
  2. Provide sensible defaults and chainable methods.
  3. Add preset factories:
    • ValidUserBuilder()
    • ClassicBookBuilder()
    • FiveStarReviewBuilder()
  4. Refactor existing tests to use builders instead of inline object setup.

Example:

book := fixtures.NewBookBuilder().
    WithTitle("The Go Workshop").
    WithAuthor("SDET Team").
    WithISBN("9780000000001").
    WithPublishedYear(2024).
    Build()

docs.title: Done Criteria
docs.text:
• Repeated test object setup is reduced.
  • Tests are easier to read and maintain.

Also ensure builder names and methods reflect domain terminology clearly.

docs.title: Deep Dive: Builder Design for Test Scalability
Background
docs.text: As tests grow, inline setup creates duplication and hides intent. Builders centralize defaults and make scenario differences explicit.

docs.title: Builder quality criteria
docs.text:
1. Safe defaults produce valid domain objects.
  2. Chainable methods express only scenario-specific variation.
  3. Preset factories map to common business scenarios.
  4. Builders avoid hidden side effects.

docs.title: Practical usage pattern
docs.text:
• Arrange: construct baseline object with default builder.
  • Customize: override only relevant fields (WithEmail, WithStatus).
  • Assert: focus on behavior under test, not object construction noise.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Keep builders in test/fixture packages and version them with domain model changes to avoid silent drift.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Using builders with invalid defaults that require constant overrides.
  • Hiding business logic inside builder methods.
  • Sharing mutable builder instances across tests.
  • Creating overly generic builders with unclear domain intent.

docs.title: Quick Builder Quality Checklist
docs.text:
• Do defaults produce valid domain entities?
  • Are chain methods focused and predictable?
  • Are preset factories aligned to common scenarios?
  • Are builders test-only and side-effect free?
  • Do tests read as behavior narratives rather than setup scripts?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Flaky Test Diagnostics.

docs.title: Embedding Playground in Pages
docs.text:
Embedding a playground is a docs-engineering task as much as a code task. Reliability depends on correct asset loading, predictable runtime behavior, and clear user feedback when execution fails.

This guide describes a practical embedding path from build output to browser execution.

docs.title: Basic Setup
docs.text: Follow setup in strict order to avoid runtime initialization issues.

docs.title: 1. Compile Go to WASM
docs.text:
GOOS=js GOARCH=wasm go build -o main.wasm cmd/main.go

Pin build scripts in repo tooling so docs builds remain reproducible.

docs.title: 2. Copy wasm_exec.js
docs.text:
cp $(go env GOROOT)/misc/wasm/wasm_exec.js .

Keep wasm_exec.js version aligned with Go toolchain version.

docs.title: 3. Create HTML Page
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>Go WASM Playground</title>
    <style>
        body { font-family: monospace; margin: 20px; }
        #output { background: #f0f0f0; padding: 10px; min-height: 50px; border-radius: 4px; }
        button { padding: 10px 20px; background: #007bff; color: white; border: none; cursor: pointer; }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <h1>Go WASM Playground</h1>

    <button onclick="runCode()">Run Code</button>
    <button onclick="clearOutput()">Clear</button>

    <h2>Output:</h2>
    <div id="output"></div>

    <script src="wasm_exec.js"></script>
    <script>
        const output = document.getElementById("output");

        function log(message) {
            output.textContent += message + "\n";
        }

        function clearOutput() {
            output.textContent = "";
        }

        async function runCode() {
            clearOutput();

            try {
                const wasmBuffer = await fetch("main.wasm").then(r => r.arrayBuffer());
                const { memory } = new WebAssembly.Memory({ initial: 256, maximum: 512 });

                const go = new Go();
                go.env = Object.assign({}, process.env);
                go.exit = code => {
                    if (code !== 0) {
                        log(`Exit code: ${code}`);
                    }
                };

                const wasmModule = await WebAssembly.instantiate(wasmBuffer, go.importObject);
                go.run(wasmModule.instance);
            } catch (err) {
                log(`Error: ${err.message}`);
            }
        }

        // Auto-run on page load
        window.addEventListener("load", runCode);
    </script>
</body>
</html>

In production pages, prefer defensive guards against multiple run triggers and repeated instantiation.

docs.title: Go Code for Playground
docs.text: Playground Go code should favor deterministic output and short execution lifecycle.

docs.title: Simple Example
docs.text:
package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "time"
)

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Welcome to Go WASM Playground!")
    fmt.Println("Current time:", time.Now().Format("2006-01-02 15:04:05"))

    // Demonstrate concurrency
    done := make(chan bool)

    go func() {
        time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
        fmt.Println("Goroutine finished!")
        done <- true
    }()

    fmt.Println("Waiting for goroutine...")
    <-done
    fmt.Println("All done!")
}
package main import ( "fmt" "time" ) func main() { fmt.Println("Welcome to Go WASM Playground!") fmt.Println("Current time:", time.Now().Format("2006-01-02 15:04:05")) // Demonstrate concurrency done := make(chan bool) go func() { time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond) fmt.Println("Goroutine finished!") done <- true }() fmt.Println("Waiting for goroutine...") <-done fmt.Println("All done!") } Run

docs.title: Advanced Embedding
docs.text: Interactive editors are useful, but they introduce extra security and resource considerations.

docs.title: Interactive Code Editor
docs.text:
<textarea id="code" style="width:100%; height:200px;">
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello from WASM!")
}
</textarea>

<button onclick="compileAndRun()">Compile & Run</button>
<div id="output" style="background:#f0f0f0; padding:10px; margin:10px 0;"></div>

<script src="wasm_exec.js"></script>
<script>
async function compileAndRun() {
    const code = document.getElementById("code").value;
    const output = document.getElementById("output");
    output.textContent = "";

    try {
        // In production, send code to server for compilation
        // then load the resulting WASM

        const wasmBuffer = await fetch("main.wasm").then(r => r.arrayBuffer());
        const go = new Go();

        const wasmModule = await WebAssembly.instantiate(wasmBuffer, go.importObject);
        go.run(wasmModule.instance);
    } catch (err) {
        output.textContent = `Error: ${err.message}`;
    }
}
</script>

docs.title: Server-Side Compilation
docs.text:
For dynamic compilation in the playground:

// server.go
package main

import (
    "net/http"
    "os"
    "os/exec"
)

func compileHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    code := r.FormValue("code")

    // Write code to temp file
    tmpfile, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "*.go")
    defer tmpfile.Close()
    tmpfile.WriteString(code)

    // Compile to WASM
    cmd := exec.Command("go", "build", "-o", "output.wasm", tmpfile.Name())
    cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GOOS=js", "GOARCH=wasm")
    cmd.Run()

    // Serve compiled WASM
    wasmData, _ := os.ReadFile("output.wasm")
    w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/wasm")
    w.Write(wasmData)
}

func main() {
    http.HandleFunc("/compile", compileHandler)
    http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil)
}
// server.go package main import ( "net/http" "os" "os/exec" ) func compileHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { code := r.FormValue("code") // Write code to temp file tmpfile, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "*.go") defer tmpfile.Close() tmpfile.WriteString(code) // Compile to WASM cmd := exec.Command("go", "build", "-o", "output.wasm", tmpfile.Name()) cmd.Env = append(os.Environ(), "GOOS=js", "GOARCH=wasm") cmd.Run() // Serve compiled WASM wasmData, _ := os.ReadFile("output.wasm") w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/wasm") w.Write(wasmData) } func main() { http.HandleFunc("/compile", compileHandler) http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil) } Run 
 
Never expose unrestricted compilation endpoints in public environments without sandboxing and abuse controls.

docs.title: Serving WASM Files
docs.text: Serving headers and MIME types are non-negotiable for reliable browser execution.

docs.title: Correct MIME Type
docs.text:
mux.HandleFunc("/wasm/", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/wasm")
    http.ServeFile(w, r, r.URL.Path[1:])
})

docs.title: With CORS
docs.text:
w.Header().Set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*")
w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/wasm")

docs.title: Performance Optimization
docs.text: Performance optimization should target both payload size and first-run interactivity.

docs.title: Use gzip
docs.text: gzip -k main.wasm

docs.title: Lazy Loading
docs.text:
async function loadWasm() {
    const response = await fetch("main.wasm.gz");
    const compressed = await response.arrayBuffer();
    const decompressed = pako.ungzip(compressed);
    return WebAssembly.instantiate(decompressed);
}

docs.title: Code Splitting
docs.text: Break large applications into smaller WASM modules loaded on demand.

docs.title: Testing Playground Code
docs.text:
Add smoke tests for boot/run/output flow whenever runtime assets or scripts change.

func TestPlaygroundCode(t *testing.T) {
    // Test code that will run in playground

    output := captureOutput(func() {
        main() // Run playground main
    })

    if !strings.Contains(output, "Hello from WASM!") {
        t.Errorf("unexpected output: %s", output)
    }
}

docs.title: Deep Dive: Robust Embedding and Runtime Safety
Background
docs.text: Embedding reliability depends on script initialization order, error visibility, and predictable module loading behavior.

docs.title: Robust embedding checklist
docs.text:
1. Load wasm_exec.js before instantiating the module.
  2. Provide explicit error messages for fetch/instantiate failures.
  3. Guard against duplicate runs from repeated button clicks.
  4. Use proper MIME type (application/wasm) on the server.

docs.title: Performance guidance
docs.text:
• Lazy-load large WASM modules when user action triggers execution.
  • Keep compiled examples small to reduce first-run delay.
  • Use compression in hosted docs environments.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Add smoke checks for playground boot, run button behavior, and output rendering to catch regressions in docs infrastructure.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Loading wasm_exec.js after module instantiation attempt.
  • Re-running the same module instance without lifecycle guards.
  • Serving .wasm without correct MIME type.
  • Exposing compile endpoints without sandbox/resource controls.

docs.title: Quick Embedding Checklist
docs.text:
• Are build artifacts generated reproducibly?
  • Is wasm_exec.js version matched to Go version?
  • Are fetch/instantiate errors clearly surfaced to users?
  • Are MIME type and caching headers configured correctly?
  • Are boot/run/output smoke checks part of docs validation?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Limitations and Best Practices.

docs.title: Limitations and Best Practices
docs.text:
WASM playground quality depends on designing with constraints, not fighting them. Go-in-browser execution is powerful for education, but runtime, security, and platform limits must shape example design.

This page helps you build examples that are reliable, understandable, and production-minded.

docs.title: WASM Limitations
docs.text: Treat limitations as explicit contract boundaries for tutorial content.

docs.title: No Direct System Access
docs.text:
// These won't work in WASM
os.Getenv()      // No environment variables
os.Open()        // No file system
net.Dial()       // Limited to same-origin requests
exec.Command()   // No process execution

Document these constraints near examples that learners might try to adapt from server-side Go.

docs.title: No OS-Specific APIs
docs.text:
// Won't work
time.LoadLocation()  // Limited timezone support
os.Signal            // No signal handling
syscall              // No system calls

docs.title: No CGO
docs.text:
// Not supported
/*
#cgo LDFLAGS: -lm
#include <math.h>
*/
// import "C"

docs.title: Single Threaded
docs.text:
• Only one goroutine can execute at a time
  • Concurrency simulated with cooperative scheduling
  • No true parallelism

Concurrency examples should focus on coordination patterns, not throughput assumptions.

docs.title: Memory Constraints
docs.text:
• Maximum 2GB addressable memory (in theory)
  • Browser typically limits to available RAM
  • No memory mapping

docs.title: Module Size
docs.text:
• Typical WASM modules: 2-5 MB uncompressed
  • After gzip: 500 KB - 1 MB
  • Cold start time: 100-500ms depending on size

Cold-start variance can significantly affect perceived docs responsiveness.

docs.title: Browser Limitations
docs.text: Browser policy constraints are a frequent source of confusion when examples involve external calls.

docs.title: Same-Origin Policy
docs.text:
// Only works for same-origin requests
resp, _ := http.Get("/api/data")       // Works
resp, _ := http.Get("https://api.example.com")  // Fails (unless CORS)

docs.title: No WebSocket (Limited Support)
docs.text: WebSocket support is browser-dependent and limited.

docs.title: No Direct File Upload
docs.text: File uploads require JavaScript bridge code.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: Best practices here aim to maximize learning value while minimizing runtime fragility.

docs.title: 1. Keep Code Small
docs.text:
// Good - focused example
func main() {
    fmt.Println("Fibonacci sequence:")
    for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
        fmt.Println(fibonacci(i))
    }
}

// Bad - complex dependencies
package main
import "mycompany.com/internal/service"

docs.title: 2. Avoid Heavy Initialization
docs.text:
// BAD - slow initialization
var complexMap = buildExpensiveMap()  // Blocks on load

// GOOD - lazy initialization
func getMap() map[string]string {
    if m == nil {
        m = buildMap()
    }
    return m
}

docs.title: 3. Optimize for Download Size
docs.text:
# Strip debug symbols
GOOS=js GOARCH=wasm go build -ldflags "-s -w" -o main.wasm

# Compress
gzip -k main.wasm

# Result
# main.wasm: 5.2 MB uncompressed
# main.wasm.gz: 800 KB compressed

docs.title: 4. Handle Async Operations Correctly
docs.text:
// Good - use channels for sync
func main() {
    result := make(chan string)

    go func() {
        time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
        result <- "done"
    }()

    fmt.Println(<-result)
}

docs.title: 5. Provide User Feedback
docs.text:
func main() {
    fmt.Println("Starting computation...")

    for i := 0; i < 100; i++ {
        if i%10 == 0 {
            fmt.Printf("Progress: %d%%\n", i)
        }
        doWork()
    }

    fmt.Println("Finished!")
}

docs.title: 6. Test Before Deployment
docs.text:
# Test locally
GOOS=js GOARCH=wasm go test ./...

# Or build and test in browser
go test -c -o test.exe

Include browser smoke checks in addition to compile-time checks.

docs.title: Common Gotchas
docs.text: Most gotchas are lifecycle and determinism issues that appear after examples grow in complexity.

docs.title: Goroutines Don't Exit Automatically
docs.text:
// BAD - infinite goroutine
func main() {
    go func() {
        for {
            time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
            fmt.Println("Running")
        }
    }()
    // Never returns, program hangs
}

// GOOD - controlled exit
func main() {
    done := make(chan bool)

    go func() {
        for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
            fmt.Println("Running", i)
            time.Sleep(1 * time.Second)
        }
        done <- true
    }()

    <-done
}
// BAD - infinite goroutine func main() { go func() { for { time.Sleep(1 * time.Second) fmt.Println("Running") } }() // Never returns, program hangs } // GOOD - controlled exit func main() { done := make(chan bool) go func() { for i := 0; i < 10; i++ { fmt.Println("Running", i) time.Sleep(1 * time.Second) } done <- true }() <-done } Run

docs.title: Time-Based Operations
docs.text:
// WASM time has limited precision
// Don't rely on exact timing for tests

// Instead use counters
counter := 0
for {
    counter++
    if counter > 1000000 {
        break
    }
}

docs.title: Memory Leaks from Goroutines
docs.text:
// Monitor for goroutine leaks
runtime.NumGoroutine()  // Returns count

docs.title: Example: Production-Ready Playground
docs.text:
package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "runtime/debug"
    "syscall/js"
)

func init() {
    debug.SetMaxStack(1 << 20)  // Limit stack
}

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Ready")

    // Expose function to JavaScript
    js.Global().Set("runAlgorithm", js.FuncOf(func(this js.Value, args []js.Value) interface{} {
        input := args[0].Int()
        result := fibonacci(input)
        return result
    }))

    // Block forever
    select {}
}

func fibonacci(n int) int {
    if n <= 1 {
        return n
    }
    return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)
}
package main import ( "fmt" "runtime/debug" "syscall/js" ) func init() { debug.SetMaxStack(1 << 20) // Limit stack } func main() { fmt.Println("Ready") // Expose function to JavaScript js.Global().Set("runAlgorithm", js.FuncOf(func(this js.Value, args []js.Value) interface{} { input := args[0].Int() result := fibonacci(input) return result })) // Block forever select {} } func fibonacci(n int) int { if n <= 1 { return n } return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2) } Run

docs.title: For Simple Concepts
docs.text:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    // One-liner examples
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}
package main import "fmt" func main() { // One-liner examples fmt.Println("Hello, World!") } Run

docs.title: For Complex Topics
docs.text:
package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "sync"
    "time"
)

func main() {
    // Show real-world pattern
    var wg sync.WaitGroup

    for i := 1; i <= 5; i++ {
        wg.Add(1)
        go func(id int) {
            defer wg.Done()
            fmt.Printf("Worker %d started\n", id)
            time.Sleep(time.Duration(id*100) * time.Millisecond)
            fmt.Printf("Worker %d finished\n", id)
        }(i)
    }

    wg.Wait()
    fmt.Println("All workers done!")
}
package main import ( "fmt" "sync" "time" ) func main() { // Show real-world pattern var wg sync.WaitGroup for i := 1; i <= 5; i++ { wg.Add(1) go func(id int) { defer wg.Done() fmt.Printf("Worker %d started\n", id) time.Sleep(time.Duration(id*100) * time.Millisecond) fmt.Printf("Worker %d finished\n", id) }(i) } wg.Wait() fmt.Println("All workers done!") } Run

docs.title: Monitoring and Debugging
docs.text: Treat observability as part of docs runtime quality, not an afterthought.

docs.title: Browser DevTools
docs.text:
• Use Chrome DevTools JavaScript console
  • Inspect WASM module in Sources tab
  • Profile execution in Performance tab

docs.title: Logging to JavaScript Console
docs.text:
import "syscall/js"

func log(message string) {
    js.Global().Get("console").Call("log", message)
}

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
• WASM is excellent for interactive documentation
  • Keep examples focused and simple
  • Test thoroughly before publishing
  • Compress for faster download
  • Document limitations for users

Use this as a decision guide for what belongs in browser playgrounds versus local project exercises.

docs.title: Background
docs.text: Interactive docs are part of the product experience. Treating playground pages like production surfaces improves trust and maintainability.

docs.title: Production-minded practices
docs.text:
1. Document unsupported APIs and runtime constraints clearly.
  2. Normalize outputs so examples remain deterministic.
  3. Add fallback messages for unsupported browser environments.
  4. Monitor bundle size and startup latency on docs pages.

docs.title: Common failure prevention
docs.text:
• Avoid long-running loops without cancellation strategy.
  • Prevent memory growth from unmanaged goroutine lifetimes.
  • Prefer explicit progress messages for longer computations.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Include browser compatibility checks in regression testing when updating WASM runtime assets.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Porting server-side examples directly without adapting for browser constraints.
  • Using long-running loops without cancellation or progress feedback.
  • Ignoring binary size growth and startup latency impact.
  • Demonstrating unsupported APIs without clear warning notes.

docs.title: Quick WASM Best-Practice Checklist
docs.text:
• Are unsupported capabilities documented where relevant?
  • Are examples deterministic and short-running?
  • Are module size and compression monitored?
  • Are user-facing errors and fallbacks clear?
  • Are browser compatibility checks part of regression flow?

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Continue with Capstone Overview.

docs.title: Playground Overview
docs.text:
The WASM playground turns static docs into interactive learning surfaces. Instead of only reading examples, learners can execute behavior immediately and validate understanding through direct feedback.

For tutorial quality, this section should define when playgrounds add value and how to keep them reliable.

docs.title: What is the WASM Playground?
docs.text:
A browser-based environment for running and testing Go code compiled to WebAssembly (WASM), directly in the documentation.

It is best used for concept exploration, not as a replacement for local development workflows.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• Interactive Learning: Run code examples directly in the docs
  • No Installation: Users don't need to install Go
  • Immediate Feedback: Execute and see results instantly
  • Embedded Examples: Keep code examples close to documentation

These benefits are strongest when examples are short, focused, and deterministic.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
1. Algorithm Examples: Show how concurrency patterns work
  2. Test Demonstrations: Run real tests in the browser
  3. API Examples: Show request/response patterns
  4. Interactive Tutorials: Build guided learning experiences

Prioritize concepts where immediate execution improves intuition (timeouts, channels, output formatting).

docs.title: How WASM Playground Works
docs.text: Understanding runtime flow helps diagnose failures when embed scripts or assets drift.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Browser
├── HTML/JavaScript
├── Go Code (compiled to WASM)
└── Runtime Environment
    └── Output Display

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
1. User clicks "Run"
  2. JavaScript loads WASM module
  3. Go code executes in browser
  4. Results displayed in output area

docs.title: Browser Compatibility
docs.text:
• Chrome 74+
  • Firefox 79+
  • Safari 14.1+
  • Edge 79+

Always communicate compatibility expectations near the playground UI.

docs.title: Go Code
docs.text:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello from WebAssembly!")
}
package main import "fmt" func main() { fmt.Println("Hello from WebAssembly!") } Run

docs.title: HTML Wrapper
docs.text:
<div id="output"></div>
<button onclick="run()">Run Code</button>
<script src="wasm_exec.js"></script>
<script>
const go = new Go();
WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(
    fetch("app.wasm"),
    go.env
).then((result) => {
    go.run(result.instance);
});
</script>

docs.title: Available APIs
docs.text: Available APIs differ from native Go runtime capabilities; document these boundaries clearly to avoid learner confusion.

docs.title: Console Output
docs.text:
• fmt.Println()
  • fmt.Printf()
  • log.Print()

docs.title: Time Operations
docs.text:
• time.Sleep()
  • time.Now()
  • time.Duration

docs.title: Concurrency
docs.text:
• Goroutines work in WASM
  • Channels work in WASM
  • However, OS-level blocking may not work as expected

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• No direct file system access
  • No network requests (same-origin only)
  • No OS-specific operations
  • No cgo (C interop)
  • Larger binary size

Treat these constraints as design inputs for example authoring.

docs.title: File Size
docs.text:
• Go WASM binary: ~2-5 MB uncompressed
  • Gzipped: ~500 KB - 1 MB
  • Plain wasm_exec.js: ~12 KB

docs.title: Performance Considerations
docs.text:
• WASM runs at native speed (after compilation)
  • Initial load time includes WASM download
  • Consider lazy loading for large apps
  • Use gzip compression in production

Measure first-run latency regularly as module size changes.

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
• WASM runs with browser sandbox restrictions
  • Cannot access host file system
  • Same-origin policy applies
  • User can inspect wasm via browser tools (not truly secret)

Do not embed secrets, tokens, or sensitive implementation details in client-delivered WASM artifacts.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Embedding Playground in Pages
  2. Limitations and Best Practices

docs.title: Deep Dive: Learning Design with WASM Playground
Background
docs.text: The playground is most effective when it reinforces one concrete concept at a time and gives immediate, observable feedback.

docs.title: Content design principles
docs.text:
1. Keep snippets short and concept-focused.
  2. Show expected output directly below the runnable block.
  3. Pair each example with one guided experiment.
  4. Avoid hidden setup so learners can reason about behavior.

docs.title: SDET recommendation
docs.text: Use playground examples to demonstrate tricky topics such as channel coordination and timeout handling, where interactive execution improves intuition.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Embedding long, multi-concept examples that obscure learning objective.
  • Using playgrounds for features that require unsupported OS/runtime capabilities.
  • Ignoring startup latency and shipping oversized modules without compression.
  • Leaving output expectations implicit, forcing learners to guess correctness.

docs.title: Quick Playground Quality Checklist
docs.text:
• Is each playground tied to one clear learning objective?
  • Are runtime limitations visible near the example?
  • Is expected output shown for comparison?
  • Are module size and load time managed?
  • Can the example run consistently across supported browsers?

docs.title: API Specification
docs.text:
The API specification is the canonical compatibility document for implementation, testing, and contract verification. If this page is precise, downstream development and CI diagnostics become significantly easier.

Treat API changes as governed changes with explicit review and test impact.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
For simplicity, this capstone uses a UserID header. In production, use JWT.

Documenting this simplification avoids confusion between tutorial constraints and production expectations.

X-User-ID: 1

docs.title: Response Format
docs.text: Consistent response envelopes are essential for consumer reliability and contract test stability.

docs.title: Success Response
docs.text:
{
  "data": {},
  "error": null
}

docs.title: Error Response
docs.text:
{
  "data": null,
  "error": {
    "code": "INVALID_INPUT",
    "message": "User not found",
    "details": {}
  }
}

docs.title: Error Codes
docs.text:
• INVALID_INPUT - 400 Bad Request
  • NOT_FOUND - 404 Not Found
  • CONFLICT - 409 Conflict
  • INTERNAL_ERROR - 500 Internal Server Error

Map these codes consistently from domain/service errors to transport responses.

docs.title: Endpoints
docs.text: Each endpoint section should define request schema, success response, and at least one representative failure.

Content-Type: application/json
